segments.s672-g89: زهرمز (written together) functions as a source phrase 'from Hormoz' (ز + هرمز). چنین باشد is a fixed poetic copular/introductory clause meaning 'is thus' or 'this is so'; اندر خبر literally 'in the report' — together: 'the report from Hormoz is thus.' Note the verbless/stative construction common in classical Persian where باشد simply colors the clause as reportative/optative rather than a full finite verb clause.
segments.s672-g90: This segment gives the content of the report in a nominal (appositive) structure: برادرت (برادر + -ت) 'your brother' with the enclitic -ت marking possession; را is the definite/object/topic marker attached to that noun; شاه ایران is the predicate 'king of Iran'; شمر is the proper name in apposition. There is no overt copula 'is'—classical Persian often links subject and predicate nominally without a finite verb.
segments.s672-g91: بتاج here is the fused prepositional phrase 'به تاج' meaning 'with a crown / crowned'; کیی‌گر (a compound/title) means 'the Kay-like king' or 'kingly person'; ننازد همی is a present-form verb with an initial negative/negative-stem particle 'ن' attached to نازَد (here used in the sense 'take pride / be proud / show off'), and همی is the archaic continuous/iterative particle meaning 'still/continually' — overall: 'though crowned as a Kay-king, he would not fail to be proud' (rhetorical question in context).
segments.s672-g92: چراخلعت stands for چرا خلعت (interrogative 'why [the] robe/gown'); از دوک 'from a spindle/bobbin' gives the material/source; سازد is a present/future-tense verb 'he makes/fashions' placed at the end as typical in Persian; همی again is the continuous particle. The literal sense: 'Why would he make a cloak out of a spindle?' — a rhetorical complaint at unkingly behaviour.
segments.s672-g93: سخن بس کن is an imperative phrase 'stop talking' or 'enough speech' (سخن 'speech' + بس 'enough' + کن imperative 'do/perform'). ازهرمز ترک زاد is a genitive/attributive construction 'of Hormoz, Turk-born' (ازهرمز 'from/by Hormoz', ترک 'Turk', زاد 'born/of origin'), giving the person about whom speech is to cease.
segments.s672-g94: که here introduces a purpose/wish clause 'that' or 'so that'; اندر زمانه 'in this age/world'; مباد is an optative/negative 'may it not be' (wishing non-existence); آن نژاد 'that lineage/stock' — literal: 'that in this age that lineage may not be,' i.e. 'may that line never exist in the world.' Note the negative optative form مباد which is common in classical Persian.
segments.s672-g95: گر 'if'; از کیقباد 'from Kay Qobad' marks the starting point for counting; اندرآری is a second-person present form 'you bring in/you reckon' (here 'you count/you start the count'); شمار 'the count/number' is the direct object — together: 'If you count from Kay Qobad...'. Note the classical conditional opener گر and the verb placed before the noun (poetic word order).
segments.s672-g96: برین here reads as بر این (written together in poetry) 'for/onto this'; تخمه 'progeny/lineage' is the thing being counted; بر سالیان 'over years' gives the temporal measure; صدهزار is the numeral 'one hundred thousand' modifying سالیان. The whole phrase means 'reckon this lineage through a hundred thousand years' (i.e. count it as lasting a hundred thousand years). Note the poetic fusion of بر + این into برین.
segments.s672-g521: This is a subordinate/clause-introducing که meaning 'that' (introducing the wish/prohibition). هرگز + مبادا is a fixed negative jussive: هرگز 'never' + مبادا 'may it not be / lest' gives a strong prohibition/wish ('may it never be'). چنین modifies the following noun ('such'), and تاجور is the noun being condemned (poetic/pejorative word). Word order is typical Persian: conjunction → adverb → jussive verb → modifier → head noun.
segments.s672-g522: کجا here functions adverbially ('anywhere' / 'where, ever') modifying the verb phrase. دست is literal 'hand' used idiomatically with یازد 'reach/lay (a hand on)' — یازد is an archaic/poetic third‑person form (compare modern یابد) expressing the potential/subjunctive after the prohibition مبادا ('may ... not reach'); note the older spelling/ending -د. به governs خون (here 'upon/to the blood') and خون پسر = 'the son's blood' (پسر = 'son').
segments.s672-g523: به گفتار is a prepositional phrase meaning 'at/with respect to the speech' or 'on hearing the words'. چون here = 'as / when' (classical usage), introducing a temporal clause. شوخ 'playful/insolent/mocking' describes گفتار; شد is past tense 'became'. لشکرش ('his army') is the subject that will act in the following clause; note the verb of this clause appears in the next segment (typical clause split).
segments.s672-g524: هم آنگه is an adverbial cluster meaning 'at once / immediately' (هم intensifies + آنگه 'then'). زدند is simple past 'they struck / set' and takes آتش ('fire') as its object; اندر درش is a locative phrase 'within its door/gate' (اندر = 'in/within', در = 'door/gate', ش = enclitic 'his/its'). So the clause says the army immediately set fire inside his gate/palace entrance.
segments.s672-g525: شدند is a past tense verb here meaning 'they came / they entered' (classical usage can use شدن for arrival/coming). اندرایوان is a compound indicating location 'in the iwan/portico'; شاهنشهی is an adjective/noun meaning 'royal/imperial' and qualifies the place. Word order: verb + locative phrase + adjective/noun in a poetic sequence.
segments.s672-g526: به نزدیک = 'to/near' (indicating movement toward proximity). آن تخت = 'that throne' (آن = 'that', تخت = 'throne'), and بافرهی is an adverbial/qualifying phrase 'with splendour / in dignity', describing the throne's state. Together they mean they approached close to that splendid throne.
segments.s672-g527: چوتاج is the poetic sequence 'چو تاج' = 'the (royal) crown' (here functioning as the object). از marks origin 'from', سرشاه literally 'the king's head' (i.e. 'from the king's head'). برداشتند is past 'they removed / they took away.' The phrase thus says they removed the crown from the king's head; note the compact, poetic ordering without additional particles.
segments.s672-g528: ز is the poetic form of 'from'. تختش = 'his throne' (تخت + enclitic ش 'his'). نگونسار is an adverb/adjective meaning 'lamenting / sorrowful / woeful' describing their mood, and برگاشتند is past 'they returned / they withdrew' — so 'they sorrowfully withdrew from his throne.' Note the compound verb برگشتن used in past plural.
segments.s672-g49: This clause uses the negative universal 'کس ... هرگز' (no one ... ever). 'کس' is the indefinite pronoun 'no one' (here negative), 'از بندگان' means 'from among the subjects/people' with 'از' as the partitive/prepositional marker, 'تاج' is the direct object 'the crown', and 'نجست' is the past form of جستن used here in the sense 'to seek/try to obtain' (classical/poetic usage). Word order is emphatic: subject/indefinite phrase + prepositional phrase + object + adverb + verb.
segments.s672-g50: The conjunction 'وگر' here means 'even if'/'but if'. 'چند' functions idiomatically as 'however (much)' or 'even though', 'بودی' is the past of 'to be' ('you/he/one was'), 'نژادش' = 'his lineage' (with enclitic -ش), and 'درست' is the adjective 'proper/correct'; the clause qualifies the preceding negative (i.e. 'not even if his lineage was good').
segments.s672-g51: 'ز' (poetic variant of از) means 'from' and introduces origin; 'ترکان' = 'the Turks'; 'یکی' here is an indefinite 'a certain'/'one', placed before the title; 'نامور' is an adjective 'renowned'; 'ساوه‌شاه' is a compound title 'the king of Saveh'. The phrase is an inverted nominal clause: 'From the Turks came a certain renowned Saveh-king.'
segments.s672-g52: 'بیامد' is the past-tense verb 'he came/arrived'. 'که' introduces a relative/result clause 'who/and so', and 'جوید' is a present-form used in epic narration to report the action 'sought' (classical narrative often uses present/present-subjunctive forms inside past narration). 'نگین و کلاه' are the objects 'gem(s) and hat' (کلاه here literal 'cap/helmet/hat').
segments.s672-g53: 'چنان خواست' is a conventional narrative formula 'thus (so) willed/desired'; 'روشن' modifies 'جهان‌آفرین' ('bright/luminous Creator' = God). 'خواست' is past tense ('willed'), so this is a statement of divine will.
segments.s672-g54: The conjunction 'که' introduces the content/result clause of the divine will. 'او' = 'he' (the previously mentioned man), 'نیست' here is negative 'is not' placed before the verb, and 'گردد' (from گشتن/گردیدن) is a subjunctive/potential 'become'/'come to be' used in classical narrative; together 'او نیست گردد' functions 'that he should not be/appear' (i.e. should not exist/remain). 'به ایران‌زمین' means 'in/to the land of Iran'.
segments.s672-g55: This nominal phrase marks the experiencer and object: 'تو را' ('to you', with را marking the person affected), 'آرزو' = 'desire/longing', and 'تخت شاهنشهی' = 'the throne of kingship/imperial throne' (تخت modifies شاهنشهی). The construction conveys 'the desire for the imperial throne (fell) to you' or idiomatically 'you desired the imperial throne.'
segments.s672-g56: 'چرا' is the interrogative 'why'. 'کرد' is the past of 'to do' and here functions with 'آرزو' to form 'چرا کرد' ≈ 'why did this happen/why did you desire'. 'زان پس که' = 'after that, since' (linking phrase), and 'بودی رهی' is past 'you were a wayfarer/traveler' (رهی = someone on the road). The whole clause asks why the wish for kingship arose given that you had been a wanderer.
segments.s672-g73: تو is the addressed pronoun (vocative/topical 'you'). پاداش آن نیکویی is the object phrase 'the recompense for that goodness' (پاداش + demonstrative آن + noun نیکویی). بد modifies the verb and here functions adverbially 'badly' / 'with evil'. کنی is the second-person present subjunctive/optative (classical Persian verb-final form) used to complete the conditional sense ('if you do ...').
segments.s672-g74: چنان دان is an imperative/hortative expression ('consider/know thus'); دان (from دانستن) is used instructively. که introduces the subordinate clause that gives the content of that consideration. بد با تن خود کنی is a subordinate clause with کنی again as the verb-final present subjunctive meaning 'you do' — بد = 'evil/bad', با + تن خود = 'to/against your own body' (با here 'with/by' used to mark relation), and خود intensifies 'your own'.
segments.s672-g75: مکن is the negative imperative ('do not'). آز را is the direct object marked with the object marker را ('greed'). برخرد is an attributive/epithet meaning 'wise' (بر- + خرد) qualifying the following noun پادشا, which is a vocative 'O king' — the phrase addresses the king and tells him not to let greed appear before the wise.
segments.s672-g76: که introduces the reason/explanation clause. دانا is the subject 'the wise man' (placed before the verb in the classical order). نخواند is a negative third-person verb form (here 'will not call / does not call' — خواندن used in the sense 'to call/name'); تو را is the object with را, and پارسا is the predicate nominal ('pious') — together 'the wise will not call you pious.'
segments.s672-g77: اگر introduces a conditional clause. من is the first-person subject. زنم here is best read as a stative/predicative use 'I am a woman' (elliptical copula), though the supplied lemma forms are from زدَن; because the provided lemma senses do not include 'woman', no sense from that list fits exactly (see senses). پند مردان دهم is a continuation where پند 'advice' is the object and دهم is the first-person present (or optative) 'I give' — 'if I (as a woman) give advice to men.'
segments.s672-g78: ببسیار سال is a temporal phrase 'for many years' (ببسیار = 'many/very', سال = 'years'). ازبرادر is a prepositional/ablative phrase 'from (a) brother' or 'by/through a brother'. کهم in the list of senses is 'little/few'; its precise syntactic role is obscure here (possibly an adverbial 'little' or a truncated word), so the literal reading is given — 'for many years, from brother, little.'
segments.s672-g79: مده is a negative imperative 'do not (give/let)'. کارکرد نیاکان is a genitive phrase 'the deeds/performance of the ancestors' (کارکرد = deeds/results, نیاکان = ancestors). بباد is a predicate phrase 'to the wind' (literally 'to the wind' = 'be lost/wasted'); the verb-like phrase follows the object in this classical ordering.
segments.s672-g80: مبادا is an invocative/negative particle 'may it not be / lest'. که introduces the subordinate clause. پند من is 'my counsel/advice' (پند + possessive من). آیدت is a compound verb form 'comes to you' (آید + enclitic ت for 'to you'), and یاد is 'memory/remembrance' — together 'lest my advice come to your memory.'
segments.s672-g465: Structure: adverbials precede the predicate — بسی (intensive adverb) + نیز (also) modify the following predicate. نزدیک is an adjectival/adverbial complement meaning "near/approaching," خسرو is the target/goal, and شدند is the 3rd person plural past of شدن used in an inchoative sense "became/grew (near)" = "approached."
segments.s672-g466: بمردانگی is a b‑prefixed adverbial/phrase meaning "with manliness" (manner). در جهان is a locative prepositional phrase "in the world," نو is an adjective "new/renewed," and شدند is again 3rd person plural past of شدن meaning "became." Together they form a secondary predication: "they became (newly) in the world by manliness / were newly notable in the world through manliness."
segments.s672-g467: چنان شد که is a fixed result clause introducer meaning "so/it came about that." از here expresses cause/reason "because of/for," بی is the privative prefix "without/lacking," شبانی is the noun "shepherd," and رمه is the affected noun "flock." Word order places the cause phrase before the result clause verb.
segments.s672-g468: پراگنده is an adjective/participle "scattered," گردد is 3rd person singular classical form of گشتن/گردیدن used here as a stative/inchoative verb "become / be made" (often translated passive), and به‌روز دمه is a temporal phrase "at the moment of day / at daybreak." The verb+temporal phrase gives the resulting event time.
segments.s672-g469: چوآگاهی آمد is a temporal clause "when news/knowledge came" (چو = when; آگاهی = news/knowledge; آمد = came). بر marks the experiencer/recipient (شهریار = the king). The whole phrase means "when the king learned / when news reached the king."
segments.s672-g470: ز introduces source/cause "about/from" (ز آیین = concerning the conduct/custom), گشسب is the personal name (Gushasp), and آنک begins a relative/pointing phrase "that one who / that which." بد نامدار is an adjectival description "ill‑famed / of bad repute." Together this is a topical apposition: "about Gushasp's conduct — that ill‑famed man."
segments.s672-g471: ز marks cause/reason "because of." تنگی here means the state of hardship/straitness, دربار is the royal court/audience, دادن in the collocation دربار دادن means "to hold court/receive petitions/hold audience," and ببست is the past tense "he closed/ceased." The clause means "because of the court's straits he stopped holding court / closed the court."
segments.s672-g472: ندیدش is the past negative construction "did not see him" (here context implies 'no one saw him'). کسی in negative contexts functions as "no one." نیز "also" links this negation to prior statements; بامی is a locative "on the roof," and بدست literally 'by/with hand' but here functions adverbially/locatively (classical usage) meaning something like "even on the roof / by the rooftop." The whole means "no one saw him, not even on the roof."
segments.s672-g169: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. بازارگان is the noun 'merchants' (indefinite group). آنک is a relative pronoun 'who/which' introducing the description. بد here is an archaic modifier used before an adjective (see note) rather than a straightforward negative; پاک مغز is a compound adjective meaning 'pure-minded' (پاک = pure, مغز = mind/brain) describing those merchants.
segments.s672-g170: سخنگوی (literally 'speaker') is used adjectivally/attributively as 'eloquent' or 'well-spoken'. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. اندرخور is an adjective meaning 'suitable/worthy of' and governs the following noun کار 'work/affair'. نغز (= نغز) qualifies کار as 'fine/skillful/delightful'; word order is noun + adjectives after the relative clause started by آنک.
segments.s672-g171: به مهر literally 'by (the) favour/kindness' — a prepositional phrase giving the motive or authority. آن درمها is 'those dirhams' (آن = that/the; درمها = dirhams, plural). ببدره درون is a locative expression; ببدره appears here as a place-name or fixed phrase and درون means 'inside/within' — together 'into the inner part / inner chamber of Bebdareh'.
segments.s672-g172: بفرمود is the polite/honorific past/simple verb 'he commanded/ordered'. بردن is the infinitive 'to take/carry' (object of the command). سوی is the preposition 'toward(s)'. طیسفون is the place-name 'Ctesiphon' and is the goal of بردن.
segments.s672-g173: بیارید here is an imperative 'bring' (contextual imperative to attendants); the lemma in the supplied list seems mismatched. پرمایه is an adjective 'rich, substantial' modifying دیبای 'brocade/silk cloth'. روم specifies origin 'of Rome/Byzantium' and is a genitival modifier of دیبای.
segments.s672-g174: که introduces a relative clause 'whose/which'. پیکر here is used metaphorically for the cloth's 'body/texture'. بریشم refers to 'fine cloth/silk' describing that texture. بد is again an archaic modifier here (see above) — not a simple negative — and joins to emphasize the quality. و is 'and'. زرش 'its gold' (= golden thread or ornament) and بوم 'homeland/native (of the loom or source)' complete the relative clause.
segments.s672-g175: بخرید is an imperative/plural command 'buy (them)'. تا introduces the purpose/result clause 'so that/in order that'. آن درم 'those dirhams' is the object, and نزدشاه means 'before the king / in the king's presence' (the intended destination of the dirhams).
segments.s672-g176: برند is a subjunctive/imperative sense 'they may take/carry' used inside the purpose clause introduced by تا. وکند is the coordinated verb phrase 'and do / and so do' (کند here is the third-person singular present of کردن used to form 'to cause / to make happen'). مهر او را — مهر 'favour/esteem' (object) + او 'his' + را object marker — names what is to be preserved. نگاه here is a noun 'regard/preservation' (from نگاه داشتن 'to keep/retain'); syntactically it completes وکند مهر او را (i.e., 'and thus keep/preserve his favour').
segments.s672-g145: This is a noun phrase giving the agent/title: سپهدار (spahbed/commander) + the proper name and epithets. In classical Persian titles and names are listed before the verb; گرد/سترگ here are name/epithet elements attached to بهرام. The whole chunk functions as the subject of the following verb.
segments.s672-g146: بفرمود is a past honorific/perfect-form verb meaning 'he commanded/ordered/said'. تا introduces a purposive/result clause here ('so that' / 'and thus'), شد is the past of شدن 'to become' (here 'became'), and دبیر بزرگ is the compound predicate 'the great/ chief scribe' (دبیر = scribe/secretary, بزرگ = great). Word order: verb بفرمود then تا + شد ... gives cause/result.
segments.s672-g147: The phrase begins with a dative/target phrase بخاقان (بـ + خاقان = 'to the khaqān/king'), followed by یکی 'a certain' modifying نامه 'letter'. ارتنگ وار is an adjectival compound (ارتنگ + وار) meaning 'in an ارتنگ manner' (here 'narrow/concise' or a particular style); وار is the suffix '-like'/'in the manner of'. The whole segment is the object phrase of the verb in the next segment.
segments.s672-g148: نبشتند is the past plural verb 'they wrote/inscribed'. What follows is a series of adjectival/qualifying phrases describing the letter: پربوی 'full of scent/ fragrant', ورنگ 'with color', ونگار 'and ornament/picture' — together indicating a richly adorned, colored inscription. Word order places the verb before its descriptive complements.
segments.s672-g149: بپوزش کنان is an adverbial participial phrase: ب- + پوزش 'with apology' and کنان 'doing' = 'making apologies / apologizing'. گفت is the past-tense reporting verb 'he said' (used here to introduce direct speech). هستم begins the reported speech ('I am...'). بدرد is an older/prepositional form (به‌دَر/بدرد) meaning 'for / of use to' or 'suitable for' and links the predicate to the following noun phrase.
segments.s672-g150: This is the predicate-complement inside the quoted speech: دلی here marks 'a heart / of heart' (i.e., 'a heart'), modified by پرپشیمانی 'full of regret/remorse'. و introduces a second item ('and'), باد سرد is a compound 'cold wind'. Together they complete the clause begun by هستم بدرد: 'I am (fit/for) a heart full of remorse and a cold wind.'
segments.s672-g151: ازین پس is a temporal phrase 'from now on / henceforth' (از + این/این variant + پس). من is the subject 'I'. آن بوم و مرز 'that land and border' is the direct object being protected; تو را is the object/topic marking 'your' with the object marker را attached to تو for emphasis/definiteness.
segments.s672-g152: نگه دارم is the present/future-minded first-person form of the compound verb نگه‌داشتن 'to keep/guard' (نگه = keeping/watch + دارم auxiliary = 'I hold/keep' / 'I will keep'). از بهر is an idiomatic prepositional compound meaning 'for the sake of' (از + بهر), ارز here means 'value/sake' (i.e., 'for your benefit/for your honour'), and تو را repeats the object/topic 'you' with را. The clause therefore states purpose/intended obligation.
segments.s672-g33: چو ... که introduces a conditional subordinate clause: چو is the conditional 'if' (archaic/poetic); خواهی is second-person present 'you want/wish'; که is the complementizer linking the desiderative to the content clause; شاهی is a noun 'kingship' (here 'to be king'); کنی is the present/subjunctive form of کردن used with شاهی to mean 'to be/make king'; بی‌نژاد is an adjective phrase 'of no noble birth' modifying the implied subject ('you').
segments.s672-g34: This main clause has an archaic inversion: داد خواهی بباد corresponds to a modern خواهی داد بباد ('you will give/you will cause to be blown away'). همه دوده را is the definite object ('all the soot' / 'everything like soot'); را is the object marker; داد is the verb stem 'gave/give' placed before the auxiliary; خواهی here functions as a future auxiliary 'you will'; بباد is a verbal phrase 'be blown away / be lost (to the wind)'.
segments.s672-g35: This temporal clause: قباد is the subject (proper name). آن زمان is an adverbial phrase 'at that time / then'; چون in this context functions causally/temporally ('when' / 'since'); بمردی is an adverbial noun 'in manhood / at maturity'; رسید is the past tense verb 'arrived / reached'. Word order is Subject — temporal phrase — verb (typical in epic/poetic lines).
segments.s672-g36: سر سوفرای is a nominal phrase where سر 'top/head' qualifies سوفرا 'table/banquet' (literally 'the top of the table' / 'on the table'). از در تاج is a prepositional phrase with از 'from/out of' and در used here as 'door/gate' (i.e. 'by the doorway of the crown/throne'); تاج is 'crown' and دید is the past verb 'he saw'. The whole phrase describes the location of the crown as perceived.
segments.s672-g37: به گفتار is a prepositional phrase 'by/under the speech/words (i.e. at the urging)'; بدگوهرانش is a possessive/agentive plural 'his bad‑gemmed ones' (i.e. his vile companions/advisers); بکشت is an archaic past verb form meaning 'he killed' or more generally 'he did [harm]' — the form and short verb stem are poetically compressed.
segments.s672-g38: This is a separate interrogative/exclamatory clause: کجا بود 'where was (there)' uses کجا 'where' and بود past copula 'was'. در پادشاهیش پشت is a locative/possessive phrase literally 'in his kingship (pādshāhī‑sh) a back/support' — پشت here means 'support/protection' and the clause asks rhetorically where such support existed in his reign.
segments.s672-g39: وزان پس is a temporal connector 'from that (time) then/after that'; ببستند is the past plural 'they bound/tied' (here 'they bound [his] feet'); پای قباد is the direct object 'Qobad's feet' (پای = 'foot/feet', followed by the name قباد). The verb is plural third person reflecting the agents who performed the action.
segments.s672-g40: This is a compact nominal phrase in apposition or description: دلاور سواری is 'a brave horseman/rider' (دلاور acting as adjective or noun modifier, سواری = 'rider'); گوی here functions as a linking/nominal particle in epic usage (rendered in translation as 'called'/'so‑named' or as a nominal tag), and کی‌نژاد is an epithet 'of noble/royal birth' describing the rider. The phrase strings descriptive nouns/adjectives typical of epic listing.
segments.s672-g489: ببندوی is an archaic 2nd-person verb form with the -وی ending (optative/future nuance); here it functions as a simple verb 'close/shut' in narrative. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and' linking the two verb phrases. گستهم precedes شد and together with شد forms a predicative unit meaning 'became/were spread' (an older/poetic verbal/adjectival element followed by the auxiliary). آگهی is the subject/object 'news/information' that is affected by 'گستهم شد'.
segments.s672-g490: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that' / 'that it was the case that'). تیره شد is a verb phrase in which تیره (adjective) takes شد 'became' to mean 'grew dark/ominous'. آن is the demonstrative 'that/the', placed before the noun phrase. فر here is the abstract noun 'farr' (royal radiance/glory) and شاهنشهی modifies it (an attributive/compound ‘of kingship/imperial’).
segments.s672-g491: همه is a universal quantifier 'all/everyone' modifying بستگان 'relatives/kinsmen' (plural). بند is the direct object meaning 'bond/fetter/tie' and برداشتند is the past plural verb 'they removed/took off' (subject = 'همه بستگان'), so the clause literally 'all the relatives removed the bonds'.
segments.s672-g492: یکی را is the indefinite/definite object phrase 'one (person) (accusative marked by را)'. بران is a locative/relational adverbial 'for that / upon that' (pointing back to the matter), and کار is the noun 'task/affair'. بگماشتند is the past plural 'they appointed/assigned', so the word order is object (یکی را) + adverbial (بران کار) + verb (بگماشتند), typical of Persian where the object can precede the verb.
segments.s672-g493: کزان is a prepositional compound 'from/of that' (k- + ز + ان), here introducing source information 'from that news'. آگهی is 'news/information' and بازجوید is a present/historical-present third-person singular verb 'he seeks/asks' (poetic tense). که چیست is an embedded interrogative clause 'what it is' (که = that/introducing clause; چی = what; ست = copula 'is').
segments.s672-g494: ز is the poetic/prepositional 'from' marking the group being referred to. جنگ آوران is a compound noun 'war-bringers' (i.e., the warriors/messengers of battle). بر در (literally 'on/at the door') with شاه 'king' specifies location 'at the king's gate'. کیست is the fused interrogative+copula 'who is (it)?' (کی = who, ست = is). The whole string asks 'who of the war-bringers is at the king's gate.'
segments.s672-g495: ز marks source/relation 'of/from' and کار زمانه is a nominal phrase 'the affairs/workings of the age' (کار = affair/work; زمانه = age/fortune). چو is the temporal conjunction 'when', آگه is the predicative/adjectival 'aware/informed', and شدند is the past plural 'they became/were', so the clause reads 'when they were informed of the affairs of the age'.
segments.s672-g496: ز فرمان 'from the command' (ز = from; فرمان = command/order) introduces what they turned away from. بگشتند is the past plural 'they returned/turned (back)' and coordinates with و 'and'. بی‌ره is an older/poetic adjective ('without way/path', variant of بی‌راه) and شدند is the copular/past 'they became', so 'they turned from the command and became without path' (i.e., bewildered or at a loss).
segments.s672-g17: رهانید is an imperative (plural or polite) meaning “release!” placed at the start; the object follows in an older/poetic order. از بند is a prepositional phrase “from the fetter/bond.” کاوس را is the definite object (کاوس = Kay Kavus) marked by را after the noun; in modern colloquial Persian the order would normally be کاوس را از بند رهانید.
segments.s672-g18: This is the object list of names to be released. همان is a demonstrative linking back to what was just mentioned (‘that/the same’); the names گیو و گودرز و هم طوس are conjoined with و, and هم before طوس adds ‘also/too’. The final را attaches to the whole phrase to mark it as the definite object of رهانید.
segments.s672-g19: همان شاه پیروز is a noun phrase with the adjective پیروز following شاه (typical older poetic order). چو here functions as a temporal conjunction 'when/as', and کشته شد is a passive/periphrastic construction: کشته (past participle) + شد (auxiliary) = 'was slain'.
segments.s672-g20: This clause reports the result: کار برگشته شد is roughly 'the affair was turned back/was reversed', with برگشته (past participle) + شد as the resultative/passive. بایرانیان here indicates the affected party or beneficiary 'for the Iranians' (older form/wording), placed before the predicate as is common in epic diction.
segments.s672-g21: دلاور شد is a copular/perfective expression 'became a hero' (دلاور as predicate noun, شد = 'became'). از here introduces the cause/reason ('because of' / 'through') and links to کار آن خوشنواز where کار (deed/act) precedes the genitival modifier آن خوشنواز ('that musician'). The sentence omits an explicit subject (understood from context).
segments.s672-g22: به آرام is an adverbial phrase 'calmly/peacefully' with به functioning adverbially; بنشست is the past tense 'sat down'. بر تخت ناز is a locative prepositional phrase 'upon the throne of n āz' (ناز here = luxury/pampering/splendor). Word order places the manner before the verb and the location after it, typical of Persian narrative.
segments.s672-g23: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. فرزند قارن is a genitival noun phrase 'the son of Qaren' (قارن here is a personal name/associate). بشد is the past/copular form 'became' or 'it came to pass that he was', and سوفرای (sufra) is a predicate noun meaning 'banquet-master/table-master' placed after the verb; the structure is a classical order where the verb follows the subject.
segments.s672-g24: که is a relative/conjunction 'who/which/that' introducing a clause describing the banquet-master. آورد is past 'brought' (he brought), and the remaining phrase گاه مهی باز جای functions as the object: گاه 'time/season', مهی 'moon-like/beautiful' (epithet), باز 'again' and جای 'place' — literally 'brought back the moon-like time to its place'.
segments.s672-g81: همه انجمن ماند زودرشگفت is a compact clause: همه انجمن (the whole assembly) is the subject, ماند is a past‑tense 3rd‑singular 'remained/stood', and زودرشگفت is an adverbial/participial phrase meaning 'soon/quickly astonished' (poetic fusion). Word order is subject + verb + adverbial; the phrase conveys that the entire assembly was quickly struck with wonder.
segments.s672-g82: سپهدار لب را بدندان گرفت: سپهدار is the subject 'the commander'; لب را is a definite object marked by را ('the lip' — را marks definiteness/emphasis); بدندان ('with the teeth') is an instrumental/adverbial phrase; گرفت is the past 3rd‑singular verb 'seized/took'. Together this forms the idiom 'to take the lip between the teeth' = 'to bite one's lip' (a bodily sign of restraint or grief).
segments.s672-g83: بدانست is past tense 'he knew/realized'. کو here is an archaic relative/indefinite 'who/which' (classical Persian uses کو where modern Persian would use 'که' or 'کی'); راست گوید همی is a verbless clause with گوید (present/habitual 3rd‑singular 'speaks/says') and همی marking continuous/habitual action, so راست گوید همی = 'who always/ever speaks truly.' The clause therefore means 'he knew who always spoke the truth.'
segments.s672-g84: جز از راه نیکی نجوید همی: جز 'except', followed by a prepositional phrase از راه نیکی 'from/the way of goodness'; نجوید is present/habitual 3rd‑singular of 'seek' and همی again gives the habitual sense. The construction with جز implies 'seeks nothing except …', so the whole segment means 'he seeks nothing but the path of goodness.'
segments.s672-g85: یلان سینه گفت: یلان (plural) 'the champions/warriors' is the subject; سینه here is instrumental/adverbial 'from the breast/heart' (i.e., speaking from the heart); گفت is the past 3rd‑plural 'they said'. ای گرانمایه زن is a vocative: ای 'O', گرانمایه 'precious/noble', زن 'woman/lady' (addressing her).
segments.s672-g86: تو درانجمن رای شاهان مزن: تو 'you' (vocative subject), درانجمن 'in the assembly' (prepositional/adverbial), رای شاهان is a genitive construction 'the counsel/opinion of kings' (رای here = advice/counsel), and مزن is a negative imperative 'do not strike/utter' (here 'do not utter/offer' your counsel). Word order is typical: you + place + object + imperative.
segments.s672-g87: که هرمز بدین چندگه بگذرد: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that/for'; هرمز is a proper name (Hormoz); بدین چندگه is a temporal phrase 'in/within these few moments' (بدین = 'in this/with this', چندگه = 'for a little while'); بگذرد (present/subjunctive/predictive form) 'will pass' or 'passes by'. Together: 'for Hormoz will soon pass by.'
segments.s672-g88: زتخت مهی پهلوان برخورد: زتخت 'from the throne' (ز + تخت), مهی is an epithet 'moon‑like/beautiful' qualifying پهلوان 'champion/hero', and برخورد is a noun/verb meaning 'encounter/meeting' or 'came upon/met'. The phrase describes a moon‑faced/beautiful champion appearing from the throne and meeting/encountering (the scene).
segments.s672-g401: ازو بستد آن نامه مرد جوان — Classical Persian word order here is verb before subject: بستد (verb) + آن نامه (object) + مرد جوان (subject). ازو is an enclitic/prepositional particle 'from him/of him' placed before the verb; بستد is a past action 'closed/tied up' (here with the sense of fastening/folding the letter). آن نامه is the direct object 'that letter', and مرد جوان is the agent 'the young man'.
segments.s672-g402: زرفتن پر اندیشه بودش روان — This is a compact, somewhat inverted clause: زرفتن ('about leaving' / here treated as a verbal noun) specifies the topic; پر اندیشه is a compound adjective 'full of thought'; بودش is past copula with enclitic pronoun 'it/was' (i.e. 'was'); روان is the subject 'spirit / soul'. Literally: 'Of leaving, full of thought was his spirit.'
segments.s672-g403: همی‌گفت زندان و بندگران — همی‌گفت is the habitual/past iterative form 'kept saying' (a frequentative past). زندان و بندگران are two coordinated nouns 'prison and the binders/jailers' that form the object of the complaining verb.
segments.s672-g404: کشیدم بدم ناچمان و چران — کشیدم is first‑person past 'I endured/suffered'. بدم is a possessive/nominal particle here ('my bad/ill‑fate' or 'my misfortune') serving as the thing endured. ناچمان 'unwilling/reluctant' and چران 'grazier/shepherd' are coordinated descriptive nouns/adjectives describing his degraded, reluctant condition (i.e. forced to tend animals).
segments.s672-g405: رهانید یزدان ازان سختیم — رهانید is a release verb which here functions optatively/imperatively in prayerful speech ('may [someone] release/deliver'); placed before یزدان (God) in the poem's inverted order. ازان = 'from that', and سختیم names the hardship/harshness being prayed against.
segments.s672-g406: ازان گرم و تیمار و بدبختیم — A list introduced by ازان 'from that': گرم ('heat' / 'ardor' / 'intense hardship'), تیمار 'care/treatment/solicitude', and بدبختیم 'my misfortune' are the items the speaker asks to be freed from; the coordinator و simply links them.
segments.s672-g407: کنون باز گردم سوی طیسفون — کنون 'now' and باز 'again' set the temporal/frame. گردم is a verb form meaning 'I turn/go/return' (first‑person sense implied); in classical/poetic Persian verb–subject–object order is flexible, so گردم precedes سوی طیسفون ('toward Ctesiphon') as the destination.
segments.s672-g408: بجوش آمد اندر تنم مغز وخون — بجوش آمد is a compound meaning 'came to boil/boiled up' (a sudden internal agitation). اندر 'within', تنم 'my body' (possessive -m), and مغز وخون 'brain and blood' are the inner things that 'boiled' — literal physiological image for intense agitation or anger.
segments.s672-g201: نه is a negation particle applying to the whole clause. آن تخمه را is the definite object (آن + noun + object marker را); تخمه is used figuratively = 'offspring/stock' and زمین after یزدان is a genitive/epithet 'of the earth' modifying تخمه. کرد is the past-tense verb 'made/caused' and یزدان is the postverbal subject (classical Persian often places the subject after the verb).
segments.s672-g202: گه آمد that‑clause: گه 'occasion/moment' + آمد 'came' = 'the time came'; که introduces the subordinate clause. برخیزد is a present/subjunctive-form verb 'to rise/get up' (3sg), and آن آفرین is a nominal phrase 'that praiseworthy one' (آفرین used as a substantive/epithet here).
segments.s672-g203: بیامد is the past tense 'arrived/came.' فرستاده is the noun 'messenger/envoy' and نیک‌پی is an epithet after it meaning 'well-born/virtuous' qualifying the envoy.
segments.s672-g204: ببغداد here marks the goal/place 'to Baghdad' (classical prepositional use). با is the preposition 'with,' and نامداران ری is an inverted noun phrase 'the renowned ones of Ray' (نامداران 'renowned, illustrious' + ری the city Rey).
segments.s672-g205: چونامه (چو + نامه = epistle/letter) is the subject. به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase 'to/near' (به + نزدیک), هرمز is the place or person-name 'Hormoz' and رسید is the past verb 'reached/arrived' (the letter reached the vicinity of Hormoz).
segments.s672-g206: رخش (the horse Rakhsh) is the subject and گشت is the past tense of گردش/گشتن 'became/turned/reacted.' زان نامه is an ablative 'from/at that letter.' چون is a comparative 'as/like' and شنبلید (a plant-name, fenugreek) is used in a simile — the clause says Rakhsh changed/reacted as if (like) a fenugreek (a poetic simile).
segments.s672-g207: پس 'then' introduces what follows. آگاهی آمد is 'information/news came' (آگاهی as noun, آمد past of 'come'). ز + مهر درم is an ablative/Source phrase 'from Mehr‑Darm' — here مهر درم functions as a compound proper name in the poem (each unit has ordinary senses but together denote the source of the news).
segments.s672-g208: یکایک 'one by one' (adverbial), بران 'upon that/thereon' (بران = بر + آن), غم is 'sorrow/grief' the object, بیفزود is the past tense 'he added/increased,' and the final غم repeats the object to emphasize 'sorrow upon sorrow' — overall: he increased that grief repeatedly.
segments.s672-g153: اگر introduces a conditional (“if”). بر + جهان پاک sets the domain: بر governs جهان (with پاک qualifying جهان as ‘whole/entire’ or ‘pure’); مهتر is a predicative noun ‘chief/leader’ and شوم is the 1st‑person present/subjunctive ‘may become / I become’, placed at the end as the main verb. The clause therefore reads literally “If over the entire world chief I become.”
segments.s672-g154: تو را is the object/topic marked with را (here marking the person to whom he speaks). همچو is a comparative particle meaning ‘like/as’. کهتر modifies برادر as ‘lesser/humble’ and شوم again is the 1st‑person subjunctive ‘I become’. Word order is SOV‑like: “to you like a lesser brother I become.”
segments.s672-g155: توباید که is an obligation construction (‘you must / you ought to’), with که introducing the subordinate clause. دل را is the definite object (‘your heart’); بشویی is the present/subjunctive of شستن used here as the action required (‘wash/cleanse’). زکین is a prepositional phrase ‘from hatred / from vengeance’, with ز- prefixed to کین (hatred) — the whole is figurative: ‘cleanse your heart of this hatred.’
segments.s672-g156: This clause states a negative existential/relational idea about the territory. نداری here functions as ‘there is not / there is no (separation)’ or expresses lack; جدا qualifies the kind of relation (‘separate’); بوم ایران is a genitive phrase ‘the land/soil of Iran’; ز چین is a prepositional phrase ‘from China’. The sense is: ‘There is no separation of Iran’s land from China’ (i.e. it should not be regarded as separate).
segments.s672-g157: چوپردخته شد is a temporal clause: چو (= when/as) + پردخته شد (passive/resultado form ‘was fashioned / was completed’). زین here refers back to the item just finished (zīn = ‘this / the saddle / trappings’ in context) and دگر ساز کرد means ‘he made another instrument/device’ (دگر = another; ساز = instrument/device; کرد = made). The verb کرد is the main past action.
segments.s672-g158: درگنج is a locative phrase ‘in the treasure / in the storehouse’. گرد آمده is a compound meaning ‘gathered / assembled’ (گرد + آمده). باز کرد is a verbal phrase ‘he opened (again/now)’ with کرد as the main verb. Together: ‘he opened the treasure in which things had been gathered.’
segments.s672-g159: سپه را marks the army as the indirect/affected party (را as an object/topic marker). درم داد is literal ‘gave dirhams / money’ (داد = gave). واسب ورهی is a coordinated phrase meaning ‘and a horse (اسب) for the way / as a mount for travel’ (the initial v- is the enclitic conjunction), so the whole clause means ‘he gave the army money and a mount for the journey.’
segments.s672-g160: نهانی is an adverb ‘secretly/hiddenly’. همی‌جست is an imperfective/past habitual form of جستن here meaning ‘he sought / was seeking’. جای مهی is a noun phrase ‘a place (جای) that is مهی’ — مهی is an epithet meaning ‘beautiful / moon‑like’, so جای مهی = ‘a lovely/suitable place’. Together: ‘secretly he looked for a fair/suitable place.’
segments.s672-g273: بباشم is a first-person singular present/subjunctive/volitive form meaning “I be / I remain” (here: “I will remain” or “let me be”). The following phrase بدین مرز is a locative expression (بدین = به + این, “at/to this”), with مرز “frontier/border”; با ایمنی is an instrumental/adverbial phrase “with safety” modifying the state expressed by the verb.
segments.s672-g274: نترسم is the negative present (ن + ترسم) of ترسیدن in 1st person singular: “I do not fear.” ز is the poetic preposition “from,” introducing the cause/object of fear; پیکار is the noun “battle,” and آهرمنی is an adjectival/attributive form referring to Ahriman or the demonic, modifying پیکار (“Ahrimanic/demonic battle”).
segments.s672-g275: چون here is the temporal conjunction “when” (classical/poetic use). شنیدند is the 3rd‑person plural past of شنیدن “they heard.” گفتار is the noun “speech/words,” and اوی is an enclitic/pronominal form meaning “his/her” (here “his”), so the phrase means “when they heard his speech.”
segments.s672-g276: همه is an indefinite/adverbial “all/everyone”; سوی is the directional preposition “toward”; آذر here is a proper name (not the element “fire”), and نهادند is the 3rd‑person plural past of نهادن (“they placed/set”), used idiomatically with روی (“face”) to mean “they turned their faces (toward ...)” — so the whole segment: “they all turned their faces toward Azar.”
segments.s672-g277: بخوردند سوگند is the idiom سوگند خوردن (lit. “to eat/take an oath”): خوردند is the 3rd‑person plural past of خوردن used idiomatically with سوگند to mean “they swore/took an oath.” زان سان is a fixed phrase “in that manner” (زان = “from/that, for that reason”; سان = “so/as”), and که خواست is a relative clause “which/that he wanted” (خواست = past of خواستن “he wanted/asked”).
segments.s672-g278: The following که introduces the content of the oath: مهرتو is مهر + تو (“your affection/favor”) with the enclitic possessive; با here is instrumental “with/by” and دیده “eye” is used idiomatically “in (our) sight/eyes.” داریم is 1st‑person plural present “we have/hold,” and راست is a predicate adjective “true,” so the clause means “that we hold your love as true/in our sight.”
segments.s672-g279: چوایمن is a fused temporal phrase (چو + ایمن) meaning “when (it/they) became safe/secure.” شد is the past of شدن “became.” از نامداران نهان: از is the preposition “from/by,” نامداران is the plural “renowned/famed ones,” and نهان is adverbial here “secretly/hidden,” indicating manner.
segments.s672-g280: ز هر سو is a common phrase “from every side” (ز = from, هر = every, سو = side). برافگند is a past 3rd‑person plural form meaning “they hurled/threw (forth)” and in martial contexts “they launched/let fly.” کارآگهان is a poetic/compound noun serving as the object, conveying sudden/urgent deeds or assaults — here “a sudden onslaught/rapid actions.”
segments.s672-g433: "چنین داد پاسخ" is literal: "thus gave an answer" — the adverbial چنين (thus) comes first, داد is the past-3sg verbal form "gave", and پاسخ is the noun "answer/reply"; که introduces the subordinate clause that follows. گرخواستی is a conditional prefixed گر- + خواستی (2sg/past or optative form) meaning "if you wished/if you wanted."
segments.s672-g434: چه کردم is a rhetorical past-tense question "what did I do" (چه + کردم, 1sg past). The following که again introduces a subordinate clause that explains/restricts the question. بدکردن is an infinitival/nominalized verb "doing evil/wrongdoing" and آراستی is an older past-form (2nd/3rd-sg-shaped) meaning "you arranged/you set up/you made into" — here it attaches to بدکردن, i.e. "that you have made (it) wrongdoing." Note the verb-final sequence in the subordinate clause (بدکردن آراستی) where the action (آراستی) qualifies the preceding noun/infinitive.
segments.s672-g435: بزد is the archaic past form "he struck/smite(d)" (3sg). گردن مهتر نامدار is a noun phrase "the neck of the famed chief/leader" with noun + modifier order (مهتر = chief, نامدار = famed) following the head; together they form the direct object of بزد.
segments.s672-g436: سرآمد here is a predicate meaning "foremost/leading." بدو is the prepositional/indirect phrase "for him / to him." بزم و هم کارزار are coordinated nouns "feast/banquet and also the battlefield," with هم adding the sense "also/together." The clause states he was foremost for him in both social and martial contexts.
segments.s672-g437: زخیمه is a noun meaning "a wound / wounded (state)" used here as a modifier: "wounded." بیاورد is a past/telic verb "he brought" (3sg). بیرون سرش is the phrase "his head out" (بیرون = out; سر + -ش enclitic = 'his head'). Word order places the descriptive noun (زخیمه) before the verb and the object phrase (بیرون سرش) after the verb.
segments.s672-g438: که introduces an explanatory/result clause. آگه نبد is an archaic negative copular construction meaning "was not aware" (آگه = aware; نبد = "was not / did not exist", negative of بودن). زان is the contracted form "of that / from that" (= از آن) modifying سخن "word/news/message." لشکرش is the possessed noun "his army/host" (لشکر + -ش). The clause thus says his host/army had not been informed/aware of that report.
segments.s672-g439: مبادا is the optative/negative particle meaning "lest / may it not be" and it governs the following clause. که introduces the clause. تنها بود نامجوی is the clause's predicate: تنها "alone" modifies نامجوی "name-/fame-seeker," and بود is the copula (here expressing the optative/wish sense after مبادا, equivalent to "be/should be"). Inversion places the predicate words after the particle for emphasis.
segments.s672-g440: بویژه که begins an explanatory remark "especially because/that..." دارد is present 3sg used here as a light verb/auxiliary meaning "he has / he is (doing)" and combines with the following directional phrase سوی جنگ روی to mean "he turns/heads toward war." سوی جنگ is a prepositional phrase "toward war," and روی (literally "face") completes the idiom "have/put one's face toward X" = "to head/turn toward X."
segments.s672-g337: بمن (to me) is a dative prepositional phrase placed before the verb. بخشد is a 3rd‑person present/jussive form used as an optative/request “may (he) grant”; او is the direct object and is explicitly marked by the object marker را. جهاندار شاه is an appositive title (“the world‑ruling king”) following the object phrase and describes the giver.
segments.s672-g338: بدان here functions as a subordinating/linking unit (literally ب + ان/دان in older usage) meaning roughly “so/so that” and introduces the result clause. تاکنون modifies the verb and here means “from now on / until now” in an older narrative sense. با من آید به راه is a clause where آید is the present/jussive “may he come” (subjunctive sense), با من = “with me,” and به راه = “on the road/along the way.”
segments.s672-g339: بدو is a prepositional pronoun “to him/unto him” (word order: indirect object first). گفت is the past tense verb “said” (in the lists given it appears as the noun ‘conversation’, but here it functions as the past verb). شاه is the subject “the king.” آن بد نابکار is a nominal phrase (that evil/wicked one) which the king names in his speech.
segments.s672-g340: به پیش تو = “in/before your presence” (به + پیش gives the locative/‘in front of’ sense; تو = ‘you’). درکی کند is an older collocation where کند is the present/predictive “will do/make” and درکی here functions as the verbal/nominative element tied to action (in older poetic usage; glossed roughly as ‘make/bring about’); کارزار is the object ‘battle/struggle’. Overall the clause predicts that the malefactor will set/bring a battle before the addressee.
segments.s672-g341: یکی مرد (...) is an indefinite nominative phrase meaning “a certain man” or “one man”: یکی = ‘a certain/one’, followed by modifiers مرد (man), خونریز (blood‑stained/blood‑thirsty), بیکار (idle/useless) and دزد (thief). Adjectives follow the head noun in this poetic word order and are linked by the conjunction و = ‘and’.
segments.s672-g342: بخواهی is a conditional-verb form “if you want/wish.” ز من = “from me” (agent/source). چشم داری is an idiomatic phrase: چشم (literally “eye/interest”) + داری (present “you have”) — here meaning “you desire/expect” or “you have an eye to/for.” بمزد is an adverbial/phrase “for payment / in return (as reward).” The whole clause says that if he wishes, (the speaker) will give such a man and the addressee will receive him from the speaker for pay/reward.
segments.s672-g343: ولیکن کنون = a classical connective “but now / however at present.” زین سخن is an older phrasing meaning “of this matter/this speech” (note زین here is an archaic demonstrative/relational form). چاره نیست is a fixed phrase “there is no remedy/solution” (نیست negates existence). Together: “But at present there is no way out of this.”
segments.s672-g344: اگر introduces a conditional “if.” زو = “from him/of him” (here written as ز + و). بتر is the comparative “worse.” نیز = “also / even so.” پتیاره is an obscure/archaic term (uncertain meaning in the lexicon), and نیست again negates. The clause conveys: “If he were even worse than that, still there would be no remedy” — i.e. no help available even in that case.
segments.s672-g345: بدو is the prepositional pronoun 'to him' (preposed here). داد is the past tense verb 'gave'. مرد بد‌آمیز is the object: مرد 'man' followed by the compound adjective بد‌آمیز ('bad‑mixing', i.e. evil‑natured); را is the definite/object marker attached to the whole object phrase. Word order is conventional older/poetic Persian: indirect object pronoun before the verb, verb in past, then the post‑nominal adjective and object marker.
segments.s672-g346: چنان is an intensifier 'such/so', modifying the following noun. بدکنش is a compound noun meaning 'ill‑doer' or 'wicked doer'. دیو خونریز is an appositive noun phrase specifying that ill‑doer: دیو 'demon' with the postpositive adjective خونریز 'blood‑shedding'. The final را again marks the (appositive) object. Together this phrase further identifies the man given in the previous segment.
segments.s672-g347: بیاورد is a narrative past (here 'brought'/'put into effect'). آیین گشسب is a genitive phrase 'the manner/order of Gushasp' (آیین = practice/custom, گشسب = the hero's name). آن سپاه 'that host/army' is the definite noun phrase (آن = 'that', سپاه = 'army') related to the action. In context this clause introduces that Gushasp organized/produced the army's order.
segments.s672-g348: همی‌راند is the imperfective/past‑progressive poetic form (همی‑ prefix + راندن) meaning 'was driving' or 'drove (continuously)'. چون باد is a simile 'like the wind' (چون = 'like', باد = 'wind') that describes the manner. لشکر is the direct noun 'army/host', and به راه = 'on/along the road' (به here = towards/into). Word order places the simile before the noun it qualifies, a normal poetic arrangement.
segments.s672-g349: بدین گونه is an adverbial phrase 'in this manner' (formed from به + این → بدین + گونه 'manner'). تا ... رسید marks the extent/destination: تا شهر همدان رسید = 'they reached as far as the city of Hamadan' (رسید is past 'arrived/reached'). The phrase orders manner then destination, typical of classical Persian narrative.
segments.s672-g350: بجایی که is the locative 'to the place where' (بجایی here corresponds to به جای/ب جای = 'to the place'), introducing a relative clause with که 'where/that'. لشکر فرود آورید is a verbal phrase 'the army (لشکر) disembarked/was brought down' where فرود is 'descent/alighting' and آورید is past 'brought' (here describing the army's alighting). The whole clause specifies the exact place they reached.
segments.s672-g351: بپرسید is a past narrative verb 'they asked/inquired'. تا here introduces the scope/destination of the inquiry ('in/to that noble city' / 'as far as that...'), and زان گرانمایه شهر is 'that noble/precious city' (زان = 'from/that', گرانمایه = 'esteemed/noble', شهر = 'city'). Together it means they made inquiries in that esteemed city.
segments.s672-g352: کسی دارد is an existential formula 'whether there is anyone / anyone has' (دارد = 'has' used as 'there is who has'). از اختر و فال بهر means 'concerning the stars and divination' — اختر 'stars' (here shorthand for knowledge of the stars, i.e. astrology), و 'and', فال 'divination/fortune‑telling'. بهر 'for'/'regarding' marks the purpose/field. The clause asks if anyone in the city possessed skill in astrology and divination.
segments.s672-g449: بدو (prepositional phrase) = 'to him/unto him' (introduces the indirect object). گفت is the reporting verb 'said' (here not listed among the provided noun senses — it functions as the verb). The following noun phrase آیین گشسب سوار is the content of what was said (a name + epithet): آیین precedes the name as an epithet, گشسب is the personal name, and سوار is a descriptive noun 'horseman/rider'. Word order here is 'to-him said [epithet name rider]'.
segments.s672-g450: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. آمد is the past tense 'came'. به جنگ = 'into/for battle' (به + noun). از در شهریار = 'from the king's gate' with از 'from' and در used in the sense 'gate/door' followed by شهریار 'the king'. The clause modifies the name and describes his arrival to battle.
segments.s672-g451: بدو گفت again 'he said to him'. The following sequence بهرام کین پارسا is a name with a qualifying epithet: بهرام (proper name), کین here is part of the name (not the common noun 'vengeance' in this context), and پارسا describes him as 'pious'. Word order: verb phrase 'said to him' then the name phrase.
segments.s672-g452: بدان is an adverbial/locative formation meaning 'to that place/there' (here segmented as ب + دان). رفته بود is the compound past (رفته + بود) giving the pluperfect/ past perfect sense 'had gone'. از در پادشا = 'from the king's gate' as in the previous clause. The auxiliary بود marks past time; رفته is the participle.
segments.s672-g453: که is a complementizer 'that' introducing reported content. با شاه = 'with the king'. ما را: ما is 'us' and را is the definite/object marker highlighting us as the beneficiary/object of the action. دهد is a present/future-oriented form of 'give' used here to mean 'will grant/make' (i.e., 'will make peace'). آشتی is the object 'reconciliation/truce'.
segments.s672-g454: بخواب 'into sleep' (here part of a subordinate idea 'to be asleep'). اندرون = 'inside/within'. سر + ش is the noun 'head/mind' with the possessive suffix 'his' (سرش = 'his head/mind'), used idiomatically for 'in his inner place/chambers'. برداشتی is second-person past of برداشتن used idiomatically: 'you took/you supposed' — i.e., 'you might have taken it (to mean)...'. The clause expresses that one might have concluded he had fallen asleep inside.
segments.s672-g455: تو is the second-person pronoun 'you'. باد here is the optative particle 'may' (classical usage), not literally 'wind' in grammatical function; together باد ... یابی forms a wish/optative. افره is the noun 'maple' (used figuratively with 'wind' as a phrase). یابی is the second-person present/subjunctive ('may you find/receive'). اکنون = 'now'. زمن = ز + من = 'from me'. The sequence expresses a wish directed at 'you' (may you now experience from me the maple-wind).
segments.s672-g456: که introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that/that'. بر تو = 'upon you' (here meaning 'for you' as the object of lament). بگریند is subjunctive/plural 'they may weep/they may lament' (used with the optative above). زار is an adverb/adjectival notion 'wretched/wailing' modifying the verb. انجمن = 'the assembly/people' who will lament. The whole clause expresses the intended/resulting lamentation of the assembly over you.
segments.s672-g505: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. بازار is the noun 'bazaar/market'. پیش here means 'in the presence of' or 'before' and governs the following noun سپاه 'host/army'. آمدند is 3rd-person plural past 'they came'. Word order is preposition + place + prepositional phrase + verb, describing source and stance before the host.
segments.s672-g506: دلاور is a plural/collective notion 'the brave/heroic ones' (noun used as subject). بدرگاه is a compound prepositional/locative noun meaning 'to the court / into the royal presence'. شاه is the possessor 'the king' (postposed to the place), and آمدند repeats the 3rd-person plural past 'they came/entered' — a second clause restating the movement into the king's presence.
segments.s672-g507: که‌گر is the conditional 'if'. گشت here carries the sense 'go / turn' (classical usage of گشتن meaning to go or set out). خواهید is 'you wish/want' (2nd-person plural/polite present), so the clause expresses 'if you wish to go'. با + مایکی is the prepositional phrase 'with Mayki' (مایکی being a proper name). Word order in older Persian places the subordinate verbs early and the auxiliary/wish after them.
segments.s672-g508: مجویید is an exhortative/imperative negative form meaning 'do not seek' (addressing you/you-pl). آزرم is a proper name (object of 'seek'), and شاه is the vocative 'O king' appended after the name as an address. اندکی modifies the clause adverbially 'for a little (while)'. The order here is negative-imperative + object + vocative + adverbial.
segments.s672-g509: که introduces an explanation/reason 'for' or 'because'. هرمز is the name 'Hormoz'. بگشته‌ست is the perfect/resultative 'has turned/changed' (ب+گشته‌ست). از here is the preposition 'from', and رای و راه is a coordination 'counsel/opinion and way/path' — together meaning 'has turned away from counsel and course'. The clause gives the reason for the following injunction.
segments.s672-g510: ازین پس is the fixed expression 'from now on' (از + این/ین + پس). مر is the enclitic/object 'to me' or 'me' (indirect object). اورا is 'him' (direct object). مخوانید is an imperative/negative-capable form of خواندن used here in the sense 'do not call/name/summon' (addressing the king), and شاه is the vocative 'O king'. The sequence therefore orders: temporal phrase + indirect object + direct object + (negative) imperative + vocative.
segments.s672-g511: بباد is an optative/subjunctive form meaning 'may it be blown away' (poetic wish). افره is a noun (a kind of tree or 'afera' here) and او is the possessive pronoun 'his/her' attached after the noun (classical order: noun + pronoun). بیازید is a jussive/optative form of یازیدن meaning 'open/burst forth' or 'be broken', and دست ('hand') follows as the affected noun. The segment uses wishes/jussives to curse or hope misfortune on the person's possessions and limb.
segments.s672-g512: برو is the imperative 'go'. بر here is a directional/prepositional element 'upon/against/at', and کنید is the 2nd-person plural present-subjunctive/imperative of کردن 'do/make' (so 'go and do / go and put upon'). آب ایران is the genitive 'the water(s) of Iran' (noun + possessor), and کبست is an obscure/archaic noun appended as the target or name (left untranslated in many editions). The clause is an imperative sequence: go + do something to/with the waters of Iran, with the final noun indicating either the object or an epithet.
segments.s672-g177: فرستاده‌ای (a messenger) is an indefinite noun phrase formed from the verb 'فرستادن' but here used as 'a messenger'; خواند is the past 3rd‑person form of خواندن used in the sense 'to call/summon' (object–verb order is normal in poetry). با شرم و هوش is an instrumental/manner phrase (‘with modesty and awareness’), where شرم is best understood as ‘modesty’ rather than accusatory ‘shame’, and هوش = ‘alertness/awareness’. The whole segment means 'he summoned a messenger, with modesty and alertness.'
segments.s672-g178: This is a nominal simile modifying the messenger: دلاور = 'valiant/hero'; بسان introduces the comparison ('like, as'); خجسته is an evaluative adjective 'auspicious/blessed'; سروش is the divine messenger/angel (here used as a model of excellence). Word order is noun + simile phrase, so 'a valiant man like the blessed Soroush.'
segments.s672-g179: یکی is an indefinite determiner 'a certain' or 'one'; نامه is the direct object 'letter'; بنوشت is an archaic/poetic past form meaning 'wrote' (from نوشتن) and takes نامه as its object. با باد و دم is an instrumental phrase 'by/with wind and breath' — a poetic expression indicating speed or that the letter/messenger rode the breeze. The sequence is Subject implied (from previous clause) + 'wrote a letter' + manner phrase.
segments.s672-g180: سخن گفت is a light verb construction: سخن 'word/speech' + گفت (here 'said/spoke') = 'he spoke words'. هرگونه modifies the manner/content 'of every kind/in any way'. ازبیش و کم is a paired phrase literally 'from more and less' = 'in greater or lesser degree' (i.e. 'more and less', 'variously'). Word order places the content phrase after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s672-g181: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' or 'of' linking this noun phrase to what precedes; پرموده is an adjective 'well‑taught/seasoned/experienced' modifying the (implied) person; و لشکر ساوه coordinates 'and the army of Saveh'; شاه is the substantive 'the king' placed after its modifiers (inverted order common in epic verse). The phrase names the king of Saveh described as experienced and accompanied by his host.
segments.s672-g182: ز رزمی uses ز ('in/of') with رزمی ('martial/in battle') to set the domain 'in battle'; کجا is an adverb 'where/somewhere' placed before the verb; کرده is the perfect/past participle 'had done' (from کردن used here in the sense 'to fare/do'); بد is an adverbial modifier 'badly/ill' qualifying کرده; با سپاه is an adjunct 'with the army' specifying the adversary or means. Together: 'somewhere in battle he had fared badly with the army.'
segments.s672-g183: وزان is a fused particle 'و + ز' meaning 'and from that' linking back to the preceding event; خلعتی is the noun 'a robe of honor' (خلعت); کآمد is an archaic past form 'came' (compound of 'آمد' with the prefix ک‑ used in epic diction); او را marks the beneficiary 'to him' with the object marker را; ز شاه specifies the source 'from the king'. The phrase says that a robe reached him from the king.
segments.s672-g184: This short phrase continues the list of sources: ز مقناع = 'from the cowl/veil' (مقناع); وز here in the text is the orthographic fusion of و + ز meaning 'and from' (so it functions as 'and from'); دوکدان سیاه = 'the black spinning‑house' where textiles are made (دوکدان = spindle‑shop). Grammatically these are coordinated ablative phrases indicating further items that came from those places.
segments.s672-g225: کنون is an adverbial fronting the clause (‘now’). زهر is the direct object. فرماید is the classical third‑person present/habitual form used here as ‘he commands/orders’ (an honorific/narrative present rather than simply ‘said’). The prepositional phrase از گنج شاه follows the verb and specifies source: ‘from the king’s treasure/treasury.’
segments.s672-g226: چو introduces a temporal clause here (‘when’). او is the third‑person pronoun subject. مست گردد is a verbal predicate: مست (adjective ‘drunk’) + گردد (classical present/finite ‘becomes’). شبان سیاه is the noun with its adjectival epithet following it (poetic ordering: ‘the black shepherd’).
segments.s672-g227: کنم is a first‑person verb form (‘I do’/‘I will’) introducing the speaker’s action. زهر is the object; با + می‌بجام expresses manner/means: با followed by می‌بجام (‘I pour’) means ‘I pour (it) with/into the wine’ — می‑ marks the classical present/habitual. اندرون is an adverbial locative ‘inside/within’ attached to the action.
segments.s672-g228: ازان (‘from that/thereafter’) links to the preceding deed. به کجا is the interrogative phrase ‘to/where’ (where to?). دست یازم is an idiomatic verb: دست (hand) + یازم (from یاختن, here ‘I lay hold/attack/seize’), meaning ‘shall I lay my hand (upon)’. The final به خون gives the target/goal ‘toward blood’ (i.e. to commit bloodshed). Word order is rhetorical but literally: ‘From that, to where shall I lay hand to blood?’
segments.s672-g229: ازین ساختن is a nominal/gerundial phrase (‘from this making/doing’ or ‘on/after this act’): از (from/of) + ین (orthographic variant of این ‘this’) + ساختن (the act of making/doing). حاجب is the subject (‘the chamberlain’). آگاه شد is a classical perfect/past construction: آگاه (aware) + شد (‘became’), i.e. ‘became aware’ or ‘learned.’
segments.s672-g230: برو is a simple past/imperative‑like narrative verb here (‘he went’ / ‘he departed’). خواب و آرام form a coordinated subject/affected state (‘sleep and calm/rest’). کوتاه شد is the predicate (‘became short/brief’) with شد marking the change of state in past narrative. Together: ‘he went; sleep and rest were shortened.’
segments.s672-g231: بیامد is the past narrative verb ‘he came’. دوان is an adverb/participle meaning ‘running/rushing’ (it qualifies how he came). پیش is the preposition ‘before/in the presence of’. خسرو is the addressee/name. بگفت is the past narrative ‘and he said’ (used to link speech to the action). The sequence is narrative: ‘He came running before Khosrow and said…’
segments.s672-g232: همه رازها is the direct object phrase ‘all the secrets’ (همه = all; رازها = secrets, plural). برگشاد is a prefixed perfect/past participial verb meaning ‘opened/unveiled/revealed’ (بر + گشادن). ازنهفت is an adverbial phrase ‘from hiding/from concealment’ (lit. ‘from the hidden’). The clause therefore means ‘he revealed all the secrets from concealment.’
segments.s672-g481: بماندند is a past plural verb 'they remained / were left'. زان 'from that' (archaic/zānun) marks the cause (’by/after that deed’). کار here means 'deed/affair' and گردان شگفت functions as a nominal phrase 'a host/amount of wonder' (poetic: a great astonishment remained). The whole segment describes people being left in astonishment by that action.
segments.s672-g482: همی is a classical continuous/progressive marker ('kept / continually'), here signalling an ongoing past action. هرکسی is the subject 'each person / everyone', رای دیگر is the object 'another/other counsel', and گرفت is the simple past 'took'. Word order is typical classical Persian: (adverb) + subject + object + verb.
segments.s672-g483: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. در طیسفون is the locative 'in Ctesiphon'. برشد is an older past-form 'arose/was raised' (3rd sg.), and این گفتگوی is the subject 'this conversation' (notice the verb preceding its subject, a common classical inversion).
segments.s672-g484: ازان 'from/thereupon' links the result. پادشاهی here means 'the kingdom/kingship'. بشد is past 'it became/it happened'. رنگ وبوی ('color and scent') is a fixed poetic collocation meaning 'appearance and air/character' — together they describe the resulting change in the kingdom's aspect.
segments.s672-g485: سربندگان is a classical collective for the leading men/courtiers. پرشد is a past/passive-like 'was filled' (lit. 'filled up'), and از indicates cause 'with/because of'. درد و کین 'sorrow and hatred' are the contents of that filling: they became full of grief and vindictiveness.
segments.s672-g486: گزیدند is past 'they chose'. نفرینش 'his/that curse' (نفس with -ش possessive) is the chosen object. بر here marks preference/contrast 'over / rather than', and آفرین means 'praise/acclamation' (so: 'they chose curse over praise'). The clause expresses preference rather than a physical placement.
segments.s672-g487: سپاه 'army/force' is the subject; اندکی is a limiting modifier 'a little / a few'. بد here functions as an evaluative/adverbial 'ill / scantily' (poetically indicating diminution or poor showing). بدرگاه 'at the presence/royal court' gives the location. The clause is elliptical (no explicit copula) and describes the army being reduced or poorly represented at court.
segments.s672-g488: جهان تنگ شد is a simple past clause 'the world became narrow' (figurative). بر دل شاه 'upon the king's heart' expresses the experiencer/recipient of that feeling. The final بر is a repeated prepositional particle used for rhythm/emphasis in classical verse — the construction means 'the king felt as if the world had grown small'.
segments.s672-g305: بدو گفت شاه این نه کارمنست is a short reported clause: بدو (to him) is a prepositional/indirect-object phrase placed before the verb for emphasis; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (poetic word-order here places the verb before the subject شاه 'the king'). این نه کارمنست is a fused archaic copular phrase: این (this) + نه (not) + کارمنست (کار من است 'is my doing') where کارمنست is a contracted/poetic form meaning 'is my doing' or 'it is my business'.
segments.s672-g306: که here is a complementizer 'that' introducing the explanation/quote; این رای (this counsel/opinion) is the noun phrase, where رای functions as 'counsel/plan'. بدگوهر آهرمنست is a poetic predicate: بدگوهر 'of bad/guilty nature' modifies the counsel and آهرمنست (literally 'is Ahriman' or 'is demonic') is an emphatic predicate attributing the evil origin — a terse way to say 'that advice is demonic/evil.'
segments.s672-g307: سپاهی فرستم places the object (سپاهی 'an army/forces') before the verb فرستم 'I will send / I will dispatch' (poetic present/future sense). The following clause تو سالار باش is an imperative: تو (you) + سالار (commander) + باش (be!), i.e. 'you be the commander.' The two clauses are coordinated: first the promise to send troops, then the instruction to the addressee.
segments.s672-g308: برزم اندرون is a short locative/adverbial phrase: اندرون 'inside / inner quarters' marks the domain; برزم is an archaic/poetic element indicating the place/field of action (here functioning as part of the locative phrase). دست‌بردار باش is an imperative compound: دست‌بردار 'desist / stop meddling' + باش 'be!' = 'refrain / cease (your meddling).' Together the clause commands the addressee to refrain from intervening in the inner affairs.
segments.s672-g309: نخستین is an adverbial/ordinal 'first(ly)'. فرستیش is a poetic/archaic verb form meaning 'send (him/it)' or 'dispatch' (object expressed after it here), and یک رهنمون is the direct object 'a guide' (رهنمون = 'one who guides' or 'guidance'). The segment functions as a command/plan: 'First, send a guide.'
segments.s672-g310: بدان here groups as a prepositional/recipient phrase 'to him' (ب- prefix + an outcome form) introducing the indirect object of sending; تا introduces a purpose clause 'so that / in order that'. چه بینی is an embedded clause 'what you see' with بینی as 2nd-person present/subjunctive/indicative 'you may see/you see' (used to express the potential/result). به سرش اندرون is a locative-figurative phrase 'into his head/within his mind' with سرش 'his inner thought' and اندرون 'inside.' Overall: 'send a guide to him so that you/he may learn what is in his mind.'
segments.s672-g311: اگر is the conditional 'if'. مهتری جوید is a compact clause: مهتری 'preeminence/supremacy' as object and جوید (poetic present/subjunctive) 'seeks/strives for' — together 'if he seeks supremacy'. تاج و تخت is a coordinated noun phrase 'crown and throne' serving as the objects/ambitions he seeks. The verb جوید is present-tense/formal-archaic used to express potential action.
segments.s672-g312: بپیچد here is a present/subjunctive-like verb 'turns/attempts/strives (to take)' or more literally 'turns/twists' — used with conditional force continuing the previous clause. بفرجام is an adverbial phrase 'in the end / at the outcome'. ازو is the preverbal genitive/agent 'from him' or 'away from him'. روی بخت is an idiomatic noun phrase 'the face of fortune' (روی = face, بخت = fortune/destiny). Together the clause means 'in the end the face of fortune will turn from him' i.e., fate will abandon him.
segments.s672-g393: نبشت is a past tense verb (he wrote) with an implied subject (he). و simply coordinates the next verb. نهاد is the past of نهادن (he placed/put), so the clause says he wrote and then placed something. از here introduces the locative/partitive relation ('on/from the face of'), followed by برش (literally 'its surface/section') and مهر خویش (مهر + enclitic ش = 'his مهر'); مهر in this context is best read as 'seal' or 'mark of affection', so the whole phrase means 'he wrote it and set his seal of love upon its face.'
segments.s672-g394: چو + شد (چو شد) forms a temporal clause 'when it became' and خشک is the predicate 'dry'. همسایه را uses را as the definite/direct-object marker ('the neighbour' as object). خواند is past tense 'he called/summoned', and پیش (here adverbial) means 'forward / into presence', so the clause means 'when it dried he summoned the neighbour forward.'
segments.s672-g395: فراوانش is an adverbial/quantifying element with the enclitic -ش referring to 'him' (i.e. 'him abundantly' / 'he to him abundantly'), so it modifies بستود ('praised'). بستود is a past tense verb 'he praised'. و coordinates to بخشید, here بخشید is transitive 'he gave/bestowed', and چیز is the direct object 'many things/gifts'.
segments.s672-g396: بسی is an intensifier here ('very/much'); برمنش is a frozen compound meaning 'concerning his disposition / noble-mindedness' (i.e. his good character). آفرین کرد is a light-verb construction where آفرین (an exclamation/word of praise) is 'made/uttered' with کرد 'he did/made', so 'he uttered much praise'. نیز at the end means 'also/too'.
segments.s672-g397: بدو is a prepositional/indirect-object phrase 'to him' (i.e. 'he said to him'). گفت is the past verb 'said' (the provided sense list does not include the verbal sense; see senses mapping). کین here functions as the demonstrative 'this' (classical کِیْن / کِن), and نامه is 'letter'; اندر نهان = 'in secret' (اندر = 'in/within', نهان = 'hidden/secretly'). Thus the clause introduces the speech: 'he said to him, "this letter, in secret..."'.
segments.s672-g398: ببرزود is a past/perfective or hortative meaning 'hasten / be quick' (here an injunction conveyed in reported speech: 'hasten'). نزدیک + شاه جهان expresses the destination/purpose: نزدیک here carries the sense 'to/near the presence of' and شاه جهان is a title 'king of the world' (a grand epithet for the king). Together: 'hasten to the presence of the king of the world.'
segments.s672-g399: چوپاسخ کند is a temporal subordinate clause 'when he answers' (چو + پاسخ + کند). زود is an adverb 'quickly'. نزد من آر is an instruction 'bring (him) to me' where نزد signals 'to the presence of / to me' and آر is an imperative form meaning 'bring' (archaic/imparative). The overall clause: 'When he answers, quickly bring him to me.'
segments.s672-g400: نگر is an imperative 'look!' or 'be careful!' (a warning). تا introduces the purpose/result clause here and functions as 'so that' (تا نباشی = 'so that you are not'). نباشی is a negative subjunctive/present ('you should not be' / 'do not be'); بر شهریار means 'before the king' / 'in the presence of the king' (بر carrying the spatial/locative sense). Together the sentence warns 'be careful so that you are not before the king.'
segments.s672-g97: که is the relative pronoun 'who/that' introducing a relative clause. با تاج is an instrumental prepositional phrase 'with crowns' placed before the verb; بودند is the past plural copula 'they were'; برتخت زر is a locative phrase (بر + تخت + زر) meaning 'upon the golden throne' with زر following تخت in classical/poetic order.
segments.s672-g98: سرآمد here means 'foremost' (an epithet/adjective). کنون is the temporal adverb 'now'. نام ایشان = 'their name' (ایشان = 'they/them'). مبر is a negative imperative 'do not take/mention' placed at the end of the clause; together the clause commands 'do not now call/mention their name as foremost.'
segments.s672-g99: ز is a poetic preposition 'about/regarding' introducing the object of thought. پرویز خسرو is the proper name 'Parviz Khosrow' (name + royal title). میندیش is a hortatory/imperative form 'think (on)!' and نیز after the verb adds 'also/too.' The object phrase precedes the verb in the classical word order.
segments.s672-g100: کزویاد is a fused classical relative/compound meaning roughly 'that/of whom/whose remembering' (کز = 'from/that' + ویاد 'of him/remembering'). کردن is the verbal noun/infinitive 'the act of doing' (here 'remembering'); نیرزد is a present negative predicate 'is not worth'; بچیز is an indefinite object 'a thing' — together: 'the remembering of him is not worth a thing.'
segments.s672-g101: بدرگاه او is a locative phrase 'in his presence / at his court' (بدرگاه = 'at court/in the royal presence', او = 'he/his'). هرک is the indefinite relative 'whoever/whichever', and ویژه‌ترند is a comparative predicate 'are more distinguished' (ویژه‌تر = 'more special/distinguished' + plural copula -ند). The clause means: 'at his court whoever is, they stand out more.'
segments.s672-g102: برادرت is برادر 'kinsman/brother' with the 2nd-person enclitic -ت 'your' (i.e. 'your kinsman/brother'). راکهتر is best read as containing the object marker را plus که‌تر (کهتر = 'the lesser/younger', comparative/relative usage) and here functions to mark 'the lesser ones'; وچاکرند is a compound 'and are (ch)ākər (servants/attendants)' with -ند plural 'they are.' Together: 'they are your kinsmen — the lesser ones and your servants.' (classical ordering and compounding are typical in epic diction).
segments.s672-g103: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بهرام is the proper name 'Bahram' (subject). گوید is the narrative/present-tense verb 'says' (the poetic/historical present common in epic narrative). بران کهتران = بر+ان (to those) + کهتران (the 'lesser/younger ones'); the phrase marks the addressees of Bahram's utterance.
segments.s672-g104: ببندند is a 3rd-person plural subjunctive/optative 'they bind' (used narratively after a command). پای + ش is the noun 'foot' (پای) with the enclitic 'ش' = 'his' ('his foot'). ببند is an imperative 'bind!' addressed to the binders, and گران is an adjective/adverb 'heavy' used here to qualify the binding ('bind heavily' / 'with heavy bonds'). The sequence describes the action carried out on his feet.
