segments.s672-g457: بفرمود is an honorific past verb 'he commanded/ordered'. داری زدن combines داری (here functioning like ‘had/caused’ or a periphrastic auxiliary meaning ‘caused to be done’) with the infinitive زدن ‘to strike/knock’, so together it means ‘had (it) struck’ or ‘ordered that (they) strike’. بر درش is a prepositional phrase: بر ‘on/upon’ + درش ‘his door/gate’ (در = door, ـش = his) marking the place where the striking/knocking is to occur.
segments.s672-g458: نظاره is a verbal noun/masdar ‘the act of looking/observing’; it functions as the activity ordered. بران is an archaic/poetic form meaning ‘upon/onto that’ (linking the watching to the following nouns). لشکر و کشورش is the thing watched: لشکر ‘army’ + و ‘and’ + کشورش ‘his country/realm’ (کشور + ـش = his).
segments.s672-g459: نگون‌بخت را is the definite object with را marking 'the ill‑fated one' (here used as a person). زنده is an adjective/adverb ‘alive’. بردار کرد is a causative/periphrastic construction: بردار (here ‘take/carry/seize’) plus کرد ‘did/caused’, so together ‘had (him) taken alive’ or ‘caused the ill‑fated one to be taken alive’. The verb کرد is the main past tense marker.
segments.s672-g460: دل مرد بدکار is a possessed noun phrase 'the heart of the wicked man' (دل = heart + مرد بدکار = the wicked man). بیدار کرد is a transitive verbal phrase 'awakened/made awake' (بیدار = awake; کرد = made/did), so the whole segment means 'he awakened the wicked man's heart.' Note Persian word order: object (دلِ مردِ بدکار) precedes the verb.
segments.s672-g461: سواران is the head noun 'horsemen/riders'. که introduces a relative clause 'who/which'. آیین here is an attributive noun 'custom/manner' or possibly part of an apposition; گشسب is the proper name Gushasp; سوار is 'rider' (appositive or attributive). The whole short phrase is the start of a relative clause describing the horsemen (ambiguous: either 'in the manner of Gushasp the rider' or 'Gushasp the rider' in apposition).
segments.s672-g462: بیاورده بود is past-perfect: بیاورده (past participle 'brought') + بود (auxiliary) = 'had brought'. از در شهریار is a prepositional phrase 'from the gate/door of the king' (از = from, در = gate/door, شهریار = king). The clause completes the relative: 'who had brought (him) from the king's gate.'
segments.s672-g463: چوکار here functions as 'the affair/what was to be done' or 'the matter at hand'; سپهبد is the title 'spahbed' (general). بفرجام شد is a fixed phrase meaning 'came to an end / reached its conclusion' (بفرجام = toward the end/at last; شد = became/happened). Together: 'when the spahbed's matter came to an end.'
segments.s672-g464: زلشکر should be read as زِ لشکر 'from the army' (the preposition زِ attached to لشکر in the manuscript form); because the provided single-token sense list does not reflect this split, its sense index is given as -1. بسی is an adverb 'many / very / a great number' (used adverbially here). پیش + بهرام شد = 'came before Bahram' or 'appeared in Bahram's presence' with شد as past auxiliary/verb. So the segment means 'many from the army came before Bahram.'
segments.s672-g25: This is a temporal clause: ز (poetic 'from/of') introduces the phrase 'ز پیروزی او' ('of his victory'); چو آمد is a subordinate temporal conjunction + past verb meaning 'when ... came/appeared'; نشان is the noun 'sign/token' that is the thing which 'came'. Word order is classical Persian: topic 'ز پیروزی او' + temporal 'چو آمد' + head noun 'نشان'.
segments.s672-g26: This main clause states the action: ز ایران 'from Iran' (ز = from) specifies source; برفتند is past tense third-person plural 'they departed/they went away'; گردن‌کشان is the subject/agent 'the neck-cutters' (a pejorative for rebels). Order: source + verb + subject (poetic inversion).
segments.s672-g27: که introduces a clause ('that/so that'); بر وی 'upon him' (بر = on/upon, وی = him) marks the target of the action; بشاهی (noun/verbal noun) 'kingship' or 'the act of making king' and کنند is third-person plural present/subjunctive 'they make/do' — together 'they make him king' or 'they proclaim him king'; آفرین here is a noun/exclamation 'acclamation/praise' referring to the acclaim that accompanies the act.
segments.s672-g28: شود is present/subjunctive/impersonal 'becomes/it happens'; کهتری is an adjective/noun 'lesser/inferior'; شهریار 'king, lord'; زمین 'earth/land' — the clause states as a grim observation that an inferior person becomes the ruler of the land. The verb شد/شود links the predicate (کهتری) to شهریار زمین.
segments.s672-g29: بایرانیان here denotes the Iranian people as the speaker ('the Iranians'); گفت is the verbal root 'said' (here represented by a noun/verb lexeme in the wordlist); کین in this clause functions as the demonstrative/particle introducing the object clause ('this/that') and ناسزاست is a predicative oxidized form meaning 'it is an insult' — the whole segment reports what the Iranians said.
segments.s672-g30: This is an explanatory clause: بزرگی 'greatness' and وتاج 'and the crown' are the topics; از پی پادشاست literally 'are from/after kingship' — از ... پی combines to indicate causal/derivative relation ('come from / follow upon'); پادشاست is the abstract/noun sense 'kingship/sovereignty' rather than a single person. Overall: 'greatness and the crown follow from being king.'
segments.s672-g31: This conditional/ concessive clause names the person Qobad (قباد) and attaches the concessive connector ارچه 'although/if'; خردست is an attributive 'is small' (خرد = small), and گردد is the present/subjunctive/optative 'becomes' or 'may become'; بزرگ is the result predicate 'great.' Word order places the concessive phrase before the result.
segments.s672-g32: نیاریم is an older/poetic first-person-plural verbal expression meaning 'we do not make/ we do not set / we will not count as a companion' (negative + root related to 'companion'); در بیشه 'in the thicket/grove' sets the location; شیر گرگ 'lion [and] wolf' are the metaphorical peers/ferocious ones. The clause says we will not admit him as a companion among the lions and wolves of the wood.
segments.s672-g537: This is a concessive conditional set-up: the repeated اگر ('if/even if') gives emphasis. بود is the past/copular form 'were/was' (here used for a hypothetical: 'were (to be) a hundred years'). The word order is inverted for poetic meter: 'صد بود سال' instead of the ordinary 'صد سال بود'. The second اگر + صدهزار continues the same concessive thought ('even if it were a hundred thousand').
segments.s672-g538: گذشت is the main verb meaning 'passed / were to pass' (used here in the hypothetical/concessive sense). آن سخن کید is a noun phrase 'that speech/tale of cunning' where کید ('plot, stratagem') qualifies سخن. اندر شمار is a prepositional phrase 'into the count / into consideration' — together: 'that crafty tale would not be reckoned (counted)'. Note the verb-front order (verb then its object phrase) is common in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s672-g539: کسی کو is a poetic/archaic relative construction meaning 'one who' (کو here functions like که = 'who'); this use is literary rather than the ordinary interrogative 'کو' ('where'). خریدار is the noun 'buyer' and نیکو شود is a 3rd-person present subjunctive/optative of شدن, 'becomes / proves to be good' — together 'one who proves a good buyer'.
segments.s672-g540: نگوید here is negative present/subjunctive (ن + گوید) meaning 'does not say / will not say' (a third-person form used in proverbial statements). سخن is the object 'a word / speech'. تا introduces the subordinate clause with an 'until' sense here. بدی نشنود: نشنود is the negative present/subjunctive of شنیدن 'to hear', so 'until he does not hear evil' — idiomatically: 'will not speak until he hears ill/evil'.
segments.s672-g113: شود here is an optative/jussive past-form verb used as a wish ('may it be/let it happen'); the phrase that follows is the thing wished for: رنج (the noun 'suffering') followed by the genitive phrase این تخمه ما (literally 'this seed of ours' = 'our line/offspring') — in classical/poetic Persian the ezāfe linking is often omitted. بباد is a predicate meaning 'be blown away / be lost' (b باد رفتن), placed at the end to complete the verb phrase ('may the suffering of our seed be blown away').
segments.s672-g114: به گفتار تو is instrumental/causal ('by/through your speech' or 'on account of your words'); کهتر بدنژاد is an insulting qualificative applied to the addressee ('inferior, ill‑born'), placed after the instrumental phrase — the whole phrase modifies the wish in the previous segment (i.e. the wished loss is said to be caused by 'your words', addressed to a low‑born person).
segments.s672-g115: کنون ('now') is a temporal adverb placed up front. راهبر باش is an imperative clause: راهبر (a noun 'guide/leader') with the copular/existential imperative باش ('be!') — together: 'be (a) guide'. بهرام را uses the object/definite marker را to indicate the target/person concerned (here 'Bahram' is the one to be guided), so the full clause means 'Now be Bahram's guide' or 'Be a guide to Bahram now.'
segments.s672-g116: پرآشوب is an adjective/participial adjective meaning 'full of turmoil' used here as the complement to the causative/do verb کن ('make/do'); کن is the imperative 'make!'. The object marked by را is the coordinated noun phrase بزم و آرام ('the feast/assembly and the calm/repose'), so the construction literally means 'make the feast and the calm tumultuous' — i.e. 'stir up the banquet and disturb the peace.'
segments.s672-g117: بگفت is simple past narrative 'he said'. این is the demonstrative object 'this' referring to what was said. وگریان (written together here) functions adverbially 'and/while weeping' (a manner participle 'گریان' with the conjunction), indicating how he spoke. سوی خانه شد is a movement phrase: سوی ('toward') + خانه ('house') + شد, where شد is used narratively/poetically to indicate motion or change of state ('turned/moved toward the house').
segments.s672-g118: به دل here functions locatively/internally ('in his heart'); با برادر 'with his brother' gives the person involved; چو biگانه شد is a simile/result clause: چو = 'as/like' and بیگانه شد = 'he became a stranger' (شد = 'became' in the past). Together the phrase says that, inwardly, he became like a stranger toward his brother (i.e. estranged in his heart).
segments.s672-g119: همی‌گفت is an iterative/habitual past 'would say' or 'kept saying' (marks repeated or customary speech). هرکس که is a generalizing relative ('whoever/anyone who' or 'everyone who/that'), introducing the clause that follows; این پاک زن is the noun phrase 'this chaste/pure woman' which functions as the subject/central referent of the relative clause.
segments.s672-g120: سخن گوی is a two‑word attributive phrase meaning 'one who speaks' / 'eloquent' (سخن = speech; گوی here is the agentive element 'speaker'). The coordinated adjectives/nominal qualities روشن‌دل ('bright/clear‑hearted') and رای زن ('womanly counsel' or 'wise in counsel' — رای = opinion/counsel, زن = woman) are listed with و; together they enumerate the woman's praised qualities. Note: in Persian poetic style such noun‑phrases may omit the ezāfe link and stack attributes.
segments.s672-g425: دلش is دل + enclitic ش (his), so literally “his heart.” پر ز + noun is a poetic idiom meaning “full of X” (پر = full, ز = from/of in classical style). اندیشه شهریار is a noun phrase “thought(s) of the king” (اندیشه modified by شهریار). The whole segment = “his heart was full of the king’s thought” (i.e. he was full of thoughts about the king).
segments.s672-g426: نگر is an imperative/exhortation “look/see/consider.” تا introduces a subordinate clause here meaning roughly “so that/that” (classical use: “see what ...”). چه is the interrogative “what.” پیش آردش is a verbal phrase: پیش آوردن (آوردن) “to bring forth” with the enclitic ش = “him,” so آرد is the present/subjunctive form used narratively for future/resultive meaning. روزگار here means “fate/fortune.” Together: “See what fate will bring him.”
segments.s672-g427: چو is a temporal conjunction “when.” همسایه آمد is a simple past clause “the neighbour came.” بخیمه درون is a locative phrase, with the older prepositional/infixal form بخیمه = ‘into/within the tent’ and درون reinforcing “inside.” Word order is classical (temporal → subject → verb → locative).
segments.s672-g428: بدانست is past tense “he knew/realized.” کو here is the archaic relative/interrogative introducing the subordinate clause (classical usage equivalent to “who/which/that”); in context it functions as “who.” دست یازد به خون is an idiomatic classical verbal phrase: دست یازیدن به +noun = “to lay hands on / to attempt” (here: to resort to bloodshed). یازد is a classical form of یازیدن. So the clause = “he knew who would lay hands to blood” (i.e. who would commit bloodshed).
segments.s672-g429: بشمشیرزد is a compact classical expression meaning “smote with the sword” (بـ instrument + شمشیر زد = struck with sword) and is a past action. دست is the direct object “hand” (what was struck or seized). خونخوار مرد is an adjectival apposition “the bloodthirsty man” (خونخوار modifying مرد). Word order places the instrument-verb before object and agent in a compressed poetic style.
segments.s672-g430: جهانجوی is the subject “the world‑seeking/ambitious man.” چندی is an adverb “for a while.” لابه کرد is an idiom meaning “made entreaties / begged” (لابه کردن = to implore); in classical Persian this appears as لابه کرد (past). برو here is a short poetic particle/prefix in this context that colors the action (continued/ongoing or directional 'toward'); in narrative it does not carry the plain modern sense “go” but is part of the idiomatic phrase. Together: “the ambitious man, for a while, begged.”
segments.s672-g431: بدو گفت is the classical pattern “he said to him” (بدو = to him/unto him; گفت = he said). کای is a vocative particle “O,” used to address the addressee. مرد گم‌کرده راه is a noun phrase “man who has lost the way” (مرد = man; گم‌کرده = lost, adjectival participle; راه = way), functioning as the person being addressed.
segments.s672-g432: نه is the negation “no / not.” من خواستم is first‑person past “I wanted / I wished” (here negated = “I did not wish”). رفته is a perfect/participial form “gone” used predicatively (“has gone” or “be taken away”). جانت is جان + enclitic ت “your life/soul” (object/possessive). ز شاه literally “from the king” (ز = from/of, شاه = king); together the clause expresses refusal of intent: “I did not wish that your life be taken from the king” (i.e. I did not intend this outcome involving the king). Word order places the negated main verb before the participial result phrase.
segments.s672-g217: بدو گفت هرمز is an inverted word order for 'هرمز بدو گفت' — 'Hormoz said to him'. بدو is the dative clitic/prepositional phrase 'to him', گفت is the past verb 'said', هرمز is the speaker's name (here the divine name). که introduces the reported clause that follows; درناگهان is an adverb 'suddenly' modifying the reported verb.
segments.s672-g218: مر این شوخ را گم کنم is a reported first‑person clause with an implicit 'I' subject (کنم is the present/subjunctive used for a future/intended action). مر is an archaic/dative/enclitic form ('to/for me' or 'm(e)') placed before the object for emphasis; این شوخ را is the definite object 'this playful fellow' (را is the object marker); گم کنم = 'I will cause (him) to be lost/vanish'. ازجهان 'from the world' specifies the manner/place of disappearance.
segments.s672-g219: نهانی is an adverb meaning 'secretly'. یکی here is an indefinite numeral 'a certain / one', modifying مرد 'man'. راخواندند is the past, plural verb 'they summoned/called', with the object مرد (را is the object marker fused with the verb form); the sequence means 'they secretly summoned a certain man.'
segments.s672-g220: شب تیره is a fronted time/scene phrase 'on a dark night' (adjective after noun is common in poetic order). با شاه بنشاندند is 'they seated (him) with the king'; بنشاندند is a transitive past 'they seated/placed', and با شاه indicates location/companionship ('beside/with the king'). The object (the summoned man) is understood from the previous clause.
segments.s672-g221: بدو گفت هرمزد is again 'to him said Hormozd' (هرمزد = Ahura Mazda). فرمان گزین is an imperative or hortative: گزین 'choose' with فرمان 'a command/order' as its object — 'choose a command' or 'give an order'. The clause is an instruction by the speaker.
segments.s672-g222: ز خسرو بپرداز is an imperative phrase addressed to the summoned man: بپرداز (here 'deal with', 'dispose of', or 'set about') takes ز خسرو 'from Khosrow' (ز = 'from', poetic). روی زمین 'on the earth' (روی = 'on/upon', زمین = 'earth/ground') specifies the sphere or manner — collectively meaning 'do away with Khosrow on the earth' (i.e. remove him).
segments.s672-g223: چنین داد پاسخ is a formulaic phrase 'thus gave answer' (چنین = 'thus', داد = 'gave', پاسخ = 'answer'). که introduces the reported speech. ایدون کنَم (ایدون کنَم) is an archaic temporal/optative phrasing: ایدون here functions as 'now / henceforth' or 'from this time', and کنَم is the first‑person present/subjunctive 'I will do' — together 'I will now ...' or 'henceforth I will ...'.
segments.s672-g224: به افسون z دل مهر بیرون کنم is the content of the promise: به افسون 'by a charm/spell' (به used adverbially 'by/with'), ز دل 'from the heart' (ز = 'from'), مهر 'love/affection' is the thing to be expelled, and بیرون کنم 'I will put out/drive out' (بیرون + کنم). Word order places instrument and source before the verb, typical of poetic Persian.
segments.s672-g361: چوآمد is a temporal clause ('when he came'); بپرسیدش is a past narrative verb phrase 'they asked him' with the clitic object -ش = 'him'; ازکارشاه is an oblique/genitive phrase 'about/from the king's affair' (literally 'from the king's work') functioning as the object of پرسید.
segments.s672-g362: وزان + کو together introduce a question of origin ('and from where'); بیاورد is the narrative/past 3rd-singular of آوردن 'he brought/led'; لشکر is the direct object 'army'; به راه is a prepositional phrase 'onto/onto the road' (به = toward/into, راه = road). The clause therefore asks 'and from where he had brought the army onto the road.'
segments.s672-g363: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو is the prepositional/clitic 'to him' placed before the verb; گفت is 'said'). ازین پس (از + این + پس) is an adverbial phrase 'from now on' or 'henceforth'. تو is the addressed subject 'you'. درگوشِ من is an adverbial phrase 'in my ear / whispered to me' (درگوش literally 'in-ear').
segments.s672-g364: یکی is an adverbial/quantifier 'one / a single'; لب بجنبان is an imperative 'move (your) lip' (بجنبان is a jussive/imperative form 'make move'); که تا begins a purpose/result clause ('so that / in order that'); هوشِ من is the phrase 'my awareness / my senses' (هوش = awareness, من = my). The clause is an instruction to whisper briefly so that his awareness (continues/recovers) — the result is completed in the next line.
segments.s672-g365: برآید is a potential/optative narrative form 'may/might rise/come up' (here expressing the hoped-for result introduced by که تا). زتیره is a prepositional phrase 'from the darkness' (ز = from; تیره = darkness/gloom) and تنم is 'my body' (تن + -م). Together the phrase means 'may my awareness rise from the darkness of my body.' The first word (ببستر) in the manuscript line is a poetic/archaic form tied into the verbal phrase and here functions within the result clause; its exact morphological role is poetically compressed.
segments.s672-g366: وگر is a conditional 'and if / but if'; خسته is a predicative adjective 'weary / exhausted' (understood of the speaker); ازخنجر is an agent/instrument phrase 'by/from the dagger' indicating cause (wounded/exhausted by the dagger); دشمنم is 'my enemy' or 'an enemy of mine' (the -م is the possessive/clitic). The clause offers an alternative: 'or if I am spent by the enemy's dagger.'
segments.s672-g367: همی‌گفت is an iterative/habitual imperfective 'he kept saying' / 'he would say' (a habitual past). با پیرزن indicates the addressee 'to/with the old woman.' راز خویش is 'his secret' (خویش = his/one's own; here split orthographically into خوی + ش), so the phrase means 'he used to tell the old woman his secret.'
segments.s672-g368: نهان کرده is a compound meaning 'had kept hidden / had concealed' (نهان = hidden; کرده = made/done, here a perfective participial construction). ازهرکس = 'from everyone' (i.e. hidden from every other person). آواز خویش = 'his voice / his spoken words' (آواز = voice/call; خویش + ش = his own), i.e. he concealed his voice/content from others while confiding in the old woman.
segments.s672-g417: ببیند is a third‑person present-form of the verb (from دیدن) with the present prefix ب‑, best read here as “he sees.” کنون (adverb) follows the verb to mark time (“now”). رای خون ریختن is the verbal noun phrase functioning as the object: رای = “plan/decision” (رای + a following verbal noun), and خون ریختن = “the shedding/spilling of blood” (خون as blood/bloodshed + ریختن as “to spill”).
segments.s672-g418: بیاساید is a stative/adverbial verbal form here meaning ‘is (or remains) restless’ (literally ‘does not find rest’ in older idiom). از رنج و آویختن is a prepositional phrase (از = “from”) with two gerundial nouns: رنج “suffering” and آویختن here as a verbal noun meaning “clinging/attachment.” Together they describe the source of his unrest.
segments.s672-g419: پراندیشه دل is a preposed adjectival/nominal phrase meaning “heart full of thought” (پراندیشه = pensive/thoughtful modifying دل). زره بازگشت places the object زره (armor) before the verb بازگشت; بازگشت here is the past action “returned / went back,” so the clause reads “with a pensive heart he returned to his armor.”
segments.s672-g420: چنان بد که is the standard correlative “so … that …” (چنان = so, بد = bad/ill). با باد marks the instrument/agent “by/with the wind.” انباز is an archaic/obscure word retained in the poem and here functions as a noun modified by the preceding phrase; گشت is the past form “became/turned” (he was turned/was made to turn). The whole clause: “so ill that, by the wind, he was turned/overwhelmed.”
segments.s672-g421: چو here is the temporal conjunction “when.” نزدیک آن نامور شد ز راه is an inverted clause meaning “that famed one drew near along the road” (نزدیک = near/approaching, آن نامور = that famed person, شد = became/came — used as the past action, and ز راه = ‘from/along the road’ in poetic ز for از).
segments.s672-g422: کسی را ندید: را is the definite/object marker and ندید is the past negative ‘did not see’; in negative contexts کسی functions as ‘anyone/no one’ (here: ‘no one’). اندر آن بارگاه is a locative phrase ‘in that court/palace’ (اندران = therein/among them, بارگاه = court).
segments.s672-g423: نشسته is a participial/stative form meaning ‘seated’ (describing posture). بخیمه is a locative phrase ‘in the tent.’ درآیین here means ‘in ceremonial/courtly manner’ or ‘in state,’ and گشسب is the proper name (Gushasp) that the clause associates with (either as possessor of the tent or as the one sitting). The order places the state and location before the identifying name.
segments.s672-g424: This is a coordinated negative inventory introduced implicitly by repeated نه: ‘neither … nor … nor …’ The nouns list what is absent: کهتر (inferior/subordinate), یاور (helper/ally), شمشیر (sword), واسب (steed/horse). The repeated نه negates each item in turn.
segments.s672-g209: بپیچید is a past narrative verb (he turned/turned aside). The conjunction و links the two verbs; شد is another past verb 'happened/came to be' used here as 'came' or 'moved' and is followed by the preposition بر + noun to indicate direction/target (بر پسر بدگمان = 'upon/toward the suspicious son'). Word order is verb → preposition phrase, typical of classical narrative.
segments.s672-g210: بگفت is an older preterite form 'he said'; it introduces what follows. این is the demonstrative 'this' (the following utterance). به آیین گشسب is a prepositional phrase 'in the manner/custom of Gushasp' (به + noun expressing manner), and آن زمان is an adverbial time phrase 'at that time' placed after the clause for emphasis.
segments.s672-g211: The clause begins with the complementizer/relative که introducing reported content. خسرو is the subject; بمردی is an abstract adverbial (ب- + مردی) meaning 'by/through manhood/manliness' and modifies رسید ('reached'). بجایی رسید is a fixed idiom 'reached a fitting/decisive point' with رسید in the past tense.
segments.s672-g212: This subordinate clause (also introduced by که) gives the consequence: از ما ('from us' = our) marks the source/target, سر is the direct object 'head', and بخواهد is a present/future modal 'will want/would demand' formed with the imperfective particle همی preceding it. کشید follows as the verbal complement meaning 'to take/dispatch/kill' (classical narrative uses such verb complexes where بخواهد + verb expresses intention or future action).
segments.s672-g213: درم را uses the definite object marker را after the object (درم) which is fronted for emphasis; همی is an emphatic/continuous particle placed before the verb; مهر سازد is a verbal compound 'to make/show affection' (سازد here is present/future 'he will make/show'), and بنیز is an enclitic adverb 'also/moreover' appended for emphasis.
segments.s672-g214: سبک داشتن is a noun + infinitive construction meaning 'the having of lightness' i.e. 'being light‑minded/frivolous' (داشتن used as a deverbal noun). بیشتر is a comparative adverb 'more/greater'. زین چه چیز is an older phrasing meaning 'than this thing' (زین = 'از این' in classical usage) with چه چیز 'what thing' used rhetorically; the whole phrase compares the magnitude/seriousness of being light‑minded to the preceding matter.
segments.s672-g215: به پاسخ is a prepositional adverbial 'in answer' or 'to reply'. چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' that typically precedes or follows گفت in classical diction; گفت is the past verb 'spoke/said', and the subject (آیین گشسب) follows the verb in the classical inverted order: 'thus spoke (the) Gushasp.'
segments.s672-g216: که introduces the quoted content. بی‌تو is the prepositional phrase 'without you'. مبیناد is a classical present-form verb meaning 'is seen/appears' (here used to say that something does not appear without the addressee). میدان و اسب are two coordinated nouns 'the field/battlefield and the horse/steed' serving as the grammatical subjects/objects of مبیناد in the quoted clause.
segments.s672-g473: برآمد is a past tense verb 'arose' with an implicit subject ('he'). ز ... وز ... is a poetic sequence meaning 'from ... and from ...' (here the second 'وز' is an archaic/poetic form representing 'و ز' = 'and from'). خورد is not a verb here but the adjective 'small/slight' modifying خواب, so ز آرام وز خورد و خواب = 'from rest and from a light sleep'.
segments.s672-g474: همی‌بود is an imperfective/past habitual copular form 'was / continued to be' describing a continuing state. با introduces the accompaniment/instrument ('with'); دیدگان is the plural noun 'eyes'; پر آب is a two-word adjectival phrase 'full of water' (poetically 'teary'). The whole phrase predicates his remaining in a teary state.
segments.s672-g475: بدربر functions adjectivally as 'ill‑fated/unfavorable' modifying سخن 'speech/talk'. سخن رفت is an idiom meaning 'talk went / talk passed' = 'people spoke' or 'the conversation turned'; چندی indicates duration 'for a while'. ز شاه here marks the topical/oblique phrase 'about/concerning the king' (classical 'ز' with overlapping senses).
segments.s672-g476: که introduces a reported clause ('that...'). پرده is the subject 'curtain'; فروهشت is a past/participial verbal form meaning 'was spread/let down'; از بارگاه is a prepositional phrase 'from the palace' marking origin. The clause reports what the talk concerned.
segments.s672-g477: یکی گفت is the stock phrase 'one said' (someone spoke); گفت is used as the speech verb. بهرام is the subject name and شد is the past verb 'became'; جنگجوی is the predicate noun 'a warrior'. Word order is typical older Persian: Subject (بهرام) + شد + predicate (جنگجوی).
segments.s672-g478: بتخت should be read as a fused prepositional expression (بِ تخت) 'to/onto the throne' (lemma here is uncertain, so marked below). بزرگی is a noun 'greatness' qualifying the throne ('great throne'); نهادست is an archaic perfective/predicative 'set/placed' (here meaning 'he set [his] face'); روی is the noun 'face' used idiomatically in 'نهادنِ روی' = 'to turn/set one's face toward' (i.e. to direct oneself toward). The phrase describes Bahram's turning toward the great throne.
segments.s672-g479: دگر گفت is 'another (person) said'; خسرو is the name subject of the reported speech. ز آزار شاه is a causal/agentive phrase 'because of the king's harm/oppression' (ز used in the causal sense here), giving the motive or circumstance in the report.
segments.s672-g480: همی is a literary particle here giving progressive/emphatic aspect ('did/indeed'), سوی ایران marks direction 'toward Iran', and گذارد is a past verb 'placed/set' used transitively with سپاه 'army' as its object — idiomatically 'he set/led an army toward Iran.' The verb comes before its object, a common older Persian order.
segments.s672-g289: کجا is the interrogative adverb 'where' placed at the start. هردو خالان is a noun phrase 'both moles' (هردو = both, خالان = moles) followed by the possessor خسرو (Khosrow) in post‑nominal position (typical of Persian: 'moles of Khosrow'). بدند is an archaic/past third‑person plural copula from بودن, here best read as 'they were/appeared' and comes at the clause end.
segments.s672-g290: بمردانگی is a noun (manliness) functioning adverbially/quality‑wise ('with manliness' or 'as manliness'). در جهان نو is a locative phrase 'in the (new) world' (در = in, جهان = world, نو = new/fresh). بدند repeats the past plural copula, linking this predication back to the moles mentioned in the previous segment.
segments.s672-g291: جزین is a restrictive phrase 'except this/except these'. هرک (classical) means 'whoever' or 'those who', and بودند is the past plural of بودن 'were'. خویشان اوی is a possessive phrase 'his relatives', where اوی is an archaic third‑person possessive 'his/her'. Together the segment means 'except those who were his relatives.'
segments.s672-g292: به زندان کشیدند is a verbal phrase 'they dragged (them) into prison' (به + place + کشیدند, کشیدند = past plural of کشیدن used transitively 'to drag/take into'). با گفت وگوی is a prepositional/adiunct phrase 'with/by discussion' — گفت وگو is the compound noun 'conversation/discussion' (here split in the word list); the phrase indicates the action was taken after or following a deliberation.
segments.s672-g293: به آیین گشسب is a prepositional phrase 'according to the custom/ritual of Gushasp' (به آیین = 'by/according to the custom', گشسب = proper name). آن زمان is a temporal phrase 'at that time'. شاه گفت places subject before verb (شاه = the king, گفت = 'said' — note the provided senses list treats گفت as noun, but here it functions as the verb 'said').
segments.s672-g294: که introduces reported content 'that'. از رای دوریم is a predicate 'we are distant from counsel/opinion' (از = from, رای = counsel/plan, دوریم = present 1st‑pl 'we are distant'). و با باد جفت is a linked predicate 'and paired with the wind' (با = with, باد = wind, جفت = paired), a metaphor meaning changeable or at the mercy of the wind.
segments.s672-g295: چو او شد is a temporal clause 'when he/that became' (چو = when, او شد = 'he became/that happened'). چه سازیم is a rhetorical question 'what shall we do' where سازیم (1st‑pl) is a present/subjunctive/optative form meaning 'shall we do/let us do'. بهرام را marks the object 'with Bahram' (را = definite/object marker).
segments.s672-g296: چنان is 'such' (demonstrative). بنده خرد is a noun + adjective 'a small‑minded/lowly servant' (بنده = servant, خرد here used in the sense 'small' or 'petty' rather than 'wisdom'). و بدکام adds the coordinating adjective 'and ill‑fated' (بدکام = ill‑fated). The trailing را again marks the object, echoing/emphasing Bahram as the thing under discussion.
segments.s672-g409: This is a short clause built around a temporal adverbial and a habitual marker: زمانی (a temporal adverb 'at one time/once') + همی (an older/historic habitual marker meaning 'would/kept') precedes the predicate phrase. بد is an attributive adjective placed before the noun (typical in classical Persian poetry) and بره is the noun 'lamb' (here figurative). بر + نژند is a prepositional phrase (بر 'on/toward' + نژند 'base/worthless') that modifies or locates the action; the whole sequence reads as a compact, slightly archaic description of repeated ill-treatment.
segments.s672-g410: پس از is a temporal prepositional sequence meaning 'after'; نامه شاه is a genitival phrase 'the king's letter' (نامه governed by از/پس). بگشاد is an archaic past/preterite form of 'گشودن' (to open) used here as a simple past 'opened'; بند is the noun 'bond/fetter/seal' functioning as the object of بگشاد (classical Persian often uses such compact noun+verb orders without an explicit object marker).
segments.s672-g411: چوآن is the subordinating temporal compound (چو + آن) meaning 'when/when that'; it introduces a temporal subordinate clause. نامه پهلوان را places نامه 'letter' with the possessor پهلوان 'of the champion' in the typical Persian order; را is the definite/object marker attached to the possessor phrase here (classical usage). بخواند is an aorist/past narrative form 'he read' (classical present/past nuance is indicated by همی in adjacent lines elsewhere).
segments.s672-g412: زکار is a prepositional phrase 'from/because of (the) deed(s)/action' (ز + کار), جهان 'the world' follows as the domain being commented on, and در شگفتی بماند is a stative result: در + شگفتی ('in astonishment'), followed by بماند (the imperfect/past-stative of ماندن) meaning 'he remained' or 'he was left' — altogether 'he remained astonished at the doings of the world.'
segments.s672-g413: که is the subordinating conjunction 'that/because'; این مرد is the noun phrase 'this man'; همسایه جانم is a classical epithet 'neighbor of my soul' (an idiomatic expression meaning intimate person or rival to one's life) with جانم ('my life') in possessive form. بخواست is the past tense of خواستن in classical form ('he sought/desired'); the clause is presented as the reason or content for the following reported speech.
segments.s672-g414: همی‌گفت is the habitual/narrative form 'kept saying' (همی marking repeated speech), کین here means 'vengeance' or 'grudge' (the object of the complaint), مهتری is an archaic noun meaning 'the chief/one of higher rank' (the target of the retribution), را is the object marker attaching to مهتری, and سزاست is a copular/evaluative predicate 'it is deserved' (classical present/past evaluative form) — together: 'he kept saying that vengeance was deserved of that chief.'
segments.s672-g415: به + خون‌م is a prepositional phrase 'for/to my blood' (خون‌م = 'my blood'); کنون‌گر is a compressed classical adverbial 'now' (کنون) with a suffix-like element indicating immediacy; شتاب is a noun 'haste/haste of action' used here idiomatically for 'impulse/urgency', and آمدش (آمد + ش) is the past form 'came to him' (the enclitic sh indicates 'to him') — together: 'now an impulse for my blood came upon him.'
segments.s672-g416: مگر functions here as a contrastive conjunction 'but/except that'; یاد is 'remembrance/memory'; زین is a classical demonstrative/relational particle (here functioning to mean 'of this' or 'from this' in context — the lemma list gives concrete senses like 'saddle' but classical poetry also uses زین as 'this/of this'); بد is the adjective 'bad/evil'; بخواب آمدش is an idiomatic verbless/resultive structure where بخواب (to sleep) plus آمدش 'came to him' means 'it came to him in sleep' or 'the memory visited him during sleep' — i.e., 'but the memory of this evil came to him in sleep.'
segments.s672-g497: شکستند is a 3rd-person plural past-form verb meaning ‘broke’/‘were broken’ (verb before object is normal in epic Persian). زندان is the direct object following the verb. و simply coordinates the two clauses. برشد is an archaic/poetic 3rd-person singular verbal form meaning ‘rose up’/‘arose’ and stands before its noun خروش (the rising of a cry); together they mean ‘a cry rose up’.
segments.s672-g498: بران سان is a fixed-style adverbial phrase meaning ‘in that manner’ or ‘so’ (بران = ‘thereon/thence’, سان = ‘like/as’). که introduces a result/clause ‘that…’. هامون is the plain/marsh (here a large plain) and برآید is the 3rd-person form ‘arises/comes up’; بجوش is a jussive/optative or presentive-used-as-resultive meaning ‘boil/seethe’ — together: ‘so that the plain begins to seethe’. Note the result clause order is typical of classical Persian poetry.
segments.s672-g499: بشهر اندرون is a locative phrase ‘within the city’ (بـ = into/in, اندرون = inner/within). هرک is the archaic fused relative ‘whoever’ (هر که). بد لشکری is a noun phrase ‘bad/ill troops’ (بد qualifies لشکری); word order places the relative before the noun phrase that it quantifies.
segments.s672-g500: بماندند is the 3rd-person plural past ‘they remained/were left’ and takes the earlier هرک ... لشکری as its subject. بیچاره is a predicative adjective ‘helpless/unfortunate’ placed after the verb (common in Persian). زان is a causal/prepositional particle ‘because of that/for that reason’ linking to ‘داوری’ (the judgment), which names the cause.
segments.s672-g501: همی‌رفت is the imperfective past marker plus verb meaning ‘was going/went’ (ongoing past). گستهم and بندوی here are personal names (subjects) moving forward; they are placed after the verb for poetic ordering. پیش functions as a preposition meaning ‘before/in the presence of’. Note that classical epic often places names and verbs in orders that differ from modern prose.
segments.s672-g502: زره‌دار is a compound meaning ‘armor-bearer/armiger’ (زره + دار 'holder'); دار here behaves as a bound element meaning ‘holder’ rather than the literal senses listed. با is the preposition ‘with’. لشکر و ساز خویش = ‘his army and his equipment’ — ساز here means ‘gear/instrument/means’ (not a musical sense), and خویش + enclitic ش (‘-ish’) marks the reflexive possessive ‘his own’ (خویش realized as خوی + ش in the tokenization).
segments.s672-g503: یکایک is an adverb meaning ‘one by one’/‘each in turn’. ز is the poetic preposition ‘from’. دیده is the noun ‘eye’ (here in the expression ‘wash from the eye’ = wipe tears). بشستند is 3rd-person plural past ‘they washed’. شرم (literally ‘shame’) is placed after the verb as the thing washed away (‘they washed shame from their eyes’ = they wiped away tears/they wept from shame).
segments.s672-g504: سواران is the subject ‘the horsemen/riders’. بدرگاه is a locative/adverbial phrase ‘to the royal presence/into the court’. رفتند is 3rd-person plural past ‘they went’. گرم here is used adverbially/adjectivally ‘fervently/eagerly’ (a manner adverb after the verb), so ‘the horsemen went eagerly into the court’.
segments.s672-g441: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when' (archaic/poetic usage). از + خون + آن + کشته forms a phrase 'from/on account of the blood of that slain (one)' with آن کشته = 'that slain [man]'. بدنام شد is a two-word predicate: بدنام (adjective 'disgraced/notorious') + شد (past/copulative 'became').
segments.s672-g442: همی‌تاخت is the imperfective/continuous past 'kept charging' (همی marking ongoing action in classical Persian). تا is the temporal 'until'; پیش + بهرام = 'before/in the presence of Bahram'. شد here functions as the verb indicating arrival/state ('came/was brought about' in context).
segments.s672-g443: بدو گفت is the idiomatic 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him/unto it', گفت = 'said' — the provided lexical senses for گفت are noun-like, so the verbal sense is not in the list). اینک is an interjectional/time adverb 'now/behold'. سردشمنت is a compact poetic form = سرِ دشمنِ‌ت 'the head of your enemy' (possessive clitic -ت implicit).
segments.s672-g444: کجا is the interrogative 'where'. The first بد functions adverbially 'badly/ill' modifying سگالیده, which is a past-participle/adjectival form 'mauled/attacked by dogs'. The second بد again intensifies/qualifies the following برتنت, which is the poetic contraction بر تنت 'on your body/garment' (برتنت = بر + تنت, with enclitic -ت 'your').
segments.s672-g445: که introduces a relative/clause 'who/that'. با لشکر آمد is straightforward 'came with an army'; همی is the classical continuous/emphatic marker attached to the verb (here conveying ongoing or emphatic aspect). پیش تو = 'in your presence/before you' (prepositional phrase placed after the verb in the poetic word order).
segments.s672-g446: نبد is an archaic negative/existential form (classical equivalent of نبود) meaning 'there was not' / 'you were not'. آگه 'aware' is the predicate; از رای = 'of the counsel/plan'. کم بیش is a fixed correlative pair 'less or more' (i.e. 'whether small or great'). تو is the emphatic/registered second-person pronoun placed at the end (typical in poetry).
segments.s672-g447: بپرسید is the simple past 'asked' (narrative third-person). The verb precedes the subject (بهرام), which is common in epic/poetic word order. کین مرد is a noun phrase 'man of kîn/vengeance' (kîn = 'vengeance', attributive before مرد), and کیست is the interrogative copula 'who is (he)?'.
segments.s672-g448: بدین here functions as the adverbial 'in/at this' (written in the source as ب + دین; the provided entry splits them, but the combination means 'in this [world/context]'). دین in the list does not carry the needed sense here (treat it as part of the deictic 'بدین', so its listed senses do not apply). سربگیتی is a poetic compound meaning roughly 'head/chief of the world' or 'this world' and is modified by the following relative clause. که introduces the relative clause; خواهد is the future/auxiliary 'will', and گریست is 'weep/weeping' (future: 'who will weep').

segments.s672-g380: همی‌کرد is the habitual/continuous past form (همی‑ prefixed to کرد), here functioning as 'would/do habitually' or 'would cause'; برخویشتن is a reflexive-directional phrase (بر + خویشتن = 'against/upon himself' → 'into himself' / 'to himself'); ناپدید functions as a resultative adjective 'vanished/absent' so the clause means 'would make him disappear/withdraw into himself.'
segments.s672-g381: که introduces the content clause 'that...'. هوش تو is a possessive phrase ('your intelligence/wit') with the possessor after the noun (classical order). بر دست همسایه‌ای is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in the power/hand of a neighbour' — note دست here means 'power/control' rather than literal 'hand', and the suffix -ای on همسایه makes it indefinite ('a neighbour').

segments.s672-g384: تو زاری کنی uses the noun زاری plus the light verb کنی (subjunctive/present of کردن) to mean 'you will lament' or 'you lament'; this periphrasis is common in classical Persian. او بریزدت خون has او 'he', then بریزد (subjunctive/optative of ریختن) with the enclitic -ت attached to the verb as the object 'you' (بریزدت = 'he spills on you' / 'he causes to pour on you'), followed by the object خون 'blood' — so 'he will spill your blood.'
segments.s672-g105: بدو (to him) is an enclitic/prepositional phrase meaning 'to him' and appears before the verb; گردیه is a past participial/verb form 'turned' (subject-action order inverted for poetry) and گفت is the speech verb 'said'. کای is the vocative particle 'O', and دیو ساز is a nominal phrase where دیو 'demon' is modified by ساز — here ساز functions poetically as 'maker/creator' (a noun following another noun).
segments.s672-g106: همی is an adverb meaning 'continually/always' placed before the verb; دیوتان is the plural 'the demons' (direct object/topic). دام سازد is a verb phrase: دام 'trap' (object) + سازد 'makes/sets' (3rd person singular present/past-tense form used narratively). براز is a noun 'dung/manure' used here as the material or a contemptuous epithet — placed after the verb for metrical/poetic effect.
segments.s672-g107: مکن is the negative imperative 'do not' placed at the start. برتن 'bar-taln' is the compound noun 'robe/garment' (object), وجام here stands as 'and cup' (written together in the manuscript form) — both items form a coordinated object followed by ما 'our/us'. بر 'by/through' plus ستم 'oppression/injustice' marks the manner ('by injustice'). segments.s672-g108: که here introduces a clause 'for/because'; از تو 'from you' (agent/source). ببینم is a 1st-person present/subjunctive form 'I see' or 'I behold' used in a generalizing statement. همی is an intensifier 'indeed/still'. باد is the optative particle 'may/let' (not 'wind' here) that softens or expresses wish/irony; و دم ('and breath') are literal nouns, so the phrase 'باد و دم' functions idiomatically as 'every passing breath/breeze' — a poetic way to say 'every little thing'.
segments.s672-g109: پدر 'father' is the subject; مرزبان 'marzban, frontier-guardian' is a predicative noun linked by the copula بود 'was' (past-tense 'was'). مارا 'us' follows to show the relation/possession ('a marzban to us' / 'our father was a marzban'); بری is an adjective or postposition here meaning 'free/for' or marking relation (in context it implies the group's standing or condition). segments.s672-g110: تو 'you' is the subject; افگندی is 2nd-person past 'you threw/caused to fall'. این جستن تخت پی is a noun phrase 'this seeking of the throne' where این 'this' modifies جستن 'search/quest' and تخت 'throne' plus پی 'after/in pursuit of' indicates that the quest is for the throne. segments.s672-g111: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; بهرام را uses را as the definite object marker marking 'Bahram' as the object; دل 'heart' is the direct object of بجوش آوری, where بجوش is an imperative/subjunctive infinitive 'let it boil' or 'to boil' used with آوری 'you cause' — together آوری causes the state: 'you cause Bahram's heart to boil' (i.e., you stirred/riled Bahram). segments.s672-g112: تبار 'lineage/folk' is the object; the following short clitic (surface 'م') and را mark possession/definiteness ('my lineage' or 'the lineage of mine') and the object marker respectively. درخروش is a locative/adverbial noun 'into uproar/clamor' and آوری (same verb) means 'you bring about' — so syntactically: 'you bring my lineage into a roar/upheaval.' segments.s672-g234: همی‌کشتن is the imperfective/continuous poetic form (همی‑ + کشتن) meaning 'was killing/attempting to kill'; او is the third-person pronoun 'him'; سگالد is a verbal/agentive notion 'setting dogs on / hunting with dogs' that describes the means; نهان is an adverb/adjective 'secretly' modifying the action. segments.s672-g134: برین is the clitic combination of بر + این 'on/for this' (i.e., 'with respect to this' or 'about this'). می‌گساریم is first-person plural present/future 'we scatter/pour' (here likely of wine or celebration) describing what they will do on that topic. لختی is an adverb 'for a short while', and بخوان is an imperative 'sing/recite' addressed to the performer — the clause switches from statement to direct command at the end.
segments.s672-g135: که introduces a subordinate clause (here causal/temporal: 'for/when'). چون شد is a classical sequence meaning 'when it came about / when it happened'. برویین is an adjectival epithet 'brazen' (literally 'of brass') modifying دز 'thief'; the adjective precedes the noun in classical usage. اسفندیار is the proper name 'Esfandiyar', completing the noun phrase 'the brazen thief Esfandiyar'.

segments.s672-g168: This clause continues the commanded actions. درم is the fee/coin 'a dirham.' مهر here means 'seal' (i.e. an impressed stamp), and برنام is functioning adjectivally/epithetically 'youthful' (likely describing the seal or its style). خسرو is used as a proper name/title ('Khosrow'). کنند is again the jussive/plural verb 'let them do / let them make.' segments.s672-g282: بدرگاه is an adverbial/place phrase 'to the (royal) presence/court'. رفتند is past tense, 3rd-person plural 'they went'. segments.s672-g283: که تا together introduce a subordinate/purposive/result clause ('so that' / 'in order that'). از marks the relation 'about/concerning' (here 'concerning his flight'), and گریزش is a nominalized stem meaning 'his flight/flight of him' (genitive with از). چه is the interrogative 'what' placed before the verb, and گوید is a 3rd-person-form of 'say' used here in an optative/indirect-future sense ('may say' / 'will say'); note the verb stands before the subject پدر (typical V–S order in classical lines): 'چه گوید پدر' = 'what the father will say'.
