segments.s672-g286: هم 'also/at once' adds immediacy ('immediately/straightaway'). اندر زمان is an adverbial phrase 'at that time' or 'in that moment'. کس is an indefinite object 'someone' (used as the person sent). فرستاد is simple past 'he sent'. The final token تفت appears after the verb as the thing/person sent; its lexical senses provided do not match a clear reading here, so its sense index is -1 (it functions here as a name or uncertain noun).
segments.s672-g287: چوگستهم is a temporal/nominal token; in classical lines such forms can be a temporal 'when X' or a name 'Gostaham'—here it introduces the time/agent. و is the coordinating 'and'. بندوی را uses the object marker را to mark بندوی as the definite object. کرد is the past tense 'did/made' (causative sense), and بند (fetter) after کرد forms a light-verb construction meaning 'made (him) fettered / bound' — the noun follows the verb, producing 'he made [someone] a captive'.
segments.s672-g288: به زندان is a prepositional phrase 'to/into prison' (به + place noun). فرستاد is the simple past 'he sent'. ناسودمند is an adjective/adverb ('unprofitable', 'of no avail') placed after the verb phrase to comment on the action — here it qualifies the sending/imprisonment as fruitless or without benefit.
segments.s672-g65: به ایران places location/scene in front of the verb in classical/poetic word order (به here functions like a locative "in/at the land of"). سوارست is an older/poetic 3rd-person present/perfect-type form meaning "is/are mounted"; the numeral phrase سیصدهزار precedes the verb (SOV-like ordering common in Ferdowsi) and denotes the quantity of riders. Together the segment reads literally "In Iran are mounted three‑hundred‑thousand."
segments.s672-g66: همه before a noun means "all/every one" and quantifies پهلوان (here used as "champion/hero"). The conjunction و simply links the two coordinated noun phrases; the repeated همه with نامدار emphasizes that every one of them is "renowned/illustrious." This is a tautologous poetic listing: "all champions and all renowned."
segments.s672-g67: همه is the subject "all/everyone." یک‌بیک here is a titular/descriptive phrase meaning "one chief/sole chieftain" modifying شاه; شاه را with the object-marker را marks the person to whom they are servants, and بنده‌اند is the present plural predicate "they are servants/they serve." The construction is literal: "All are servants of one chieftain, the king."
segments.s672-g68: بفرمان is an adverbial/prepositional phrase meaning "by (his) command/at the command (of him)"; و links it to the following noun. رایش is a possessive form meaning "his opinion/judgment/will," and سرافگنده‌اند is a plural predicate meaning "they are abashed/humiliated." Grammatically the two prepositional/nominal phrases modify the cause or manner of their being "سرافگنده" (i.e., "ashamed at his command and will").
segments.s672-g69: شهنشاه گیتی is a compound title "king/emperor of the world" (subject). تو را uses the object-marker را to mark "you" as the direct object, and برگزید is a past-tense verb "chose/selected." Word order is subject — object — verb (classical Persian tendency to put the verb at the end).
segments.s672-g70: چنان is a subordinating adverb meaning "so/such that," and کز is the contracted classical combination of که + از meaning "that/from which" introducing the subordinate clause. ره is the noun "way/path," نامداران "the renowned/illustrious," and سزید is a past-form meaning "was made worthy/it befitted/one deserved." The clause as a whole means roughly "so that, by the path of the famed, you became deserving/fit," with the verb sited at the end as typical.
segments.s672-g71: نیاکانت را is the object phrase "your ancestors" marked with را. همچنین is an adverb meaning "similarly/likewise" modifying the following verbal action. نام داد is a light verbal phrase "gave a name/bestowed a name" (نام = "name/title/fame," داد = past "gave"). The whole segment reads "He likewise gave a name to your ancestors."
segments.s672-g72: بفرجام is an adverbial phrase "at the end/ultimately." بر + دشمنان is a prepositional phrase "against/upon the enemies." کام داد is an idiomatic compound where کام ("desire/satisfaction/fulfilment") with داد ("gave") means "gave fulfilment/pleasure" or more contextually "secured satisfaction/victory (over)" — the verb comes at the end and the whole segment means "in the end he gave fulfilment against the enemies."
segments.s672-g329: مرا + گر is an archaic conditional: مرا (me, object pronoun) precedes the particle گر (‘if’), so مرا گر بخواهی = “if you want me.” بخواهی is 2nd‑person singular present/subjunctive of خواستن used here for a conditional/wish (“you want/you ask”). توازشهریار is a fused phrase (تو از شهریار) identifying the addressee as coming from or sent by the king (“you, the king’s man/agent”), placed appositionally after the condition.
segments.s672-g330: دوان (an aoristic/imperative‑flavored verb 'rush/run') expresses a quick motion and here introduces the speaker’s action. با تو آیم is the verb آیم (1st‑person present/subjunctive ‘I come’) with the preposition با ‘with’, so با تو آیم = “I will come with you.” بر این کارزار (written here as بر + ین + کارزار) means “to/onto this battle” (بر...این = toward/onto this). The clause as a whole is a promise of immediate accompaniment.
segments.s672-g331: به پیش تو is a prepositional phrase meaning “in your presence / before you” (به + پیش). جان را is the direct object marked by the postposition را (‘life’ as the thing offered). بکوشم is 1st‑person singular present/subjunctive of کوشیدن (here with ب‑ prefix), used idiomatically as a willing/future action: “I will strive/venture” (i.e. risk or give up life). به جنگ is an adverbial phrase ‘in battle’ or ‘to war’ specifying the context.
segments.s672-g332: چو is a classical temporal conjunction here meaning “when.” یابم is 1st‑person subjunctive/potential of یافتن used for a future/possible event “I obtain / I find.” رهایی is the noun “release/freedom” and ز زندان تنگ is a prepositional phrase (ز = from) with زندان modified by تنگ ‘narrow/close’ — “release from the narrow prison.” The clause gives the time condition for the action in the previous segment.
segments.s672-g333: فرستاد is 3rd‑person singular past (‘he sent’). آیین here functions as an epithet/qualifier attached to the name that follows (آیین گشسب), a poetic way of marking Gashasp as illustrious/ceremonial; آن زمان is the time adverbial ‘at that time’ placed after the verb, a normal classical word order.
segments.s672-g334: کسی را is the direct object (‘someone’, with را as the object marker). بر here means ‘to/toward’ or ‘before’ when used with a courtly verb of presentation; شاه گیتی دمان is a compound/title: شاه (king) + گیتی (world) + دمان (literally ‘hem/edge’ used poetically) giving the sense ‘the king of the world’ (a grand epithet). Thus the clause says Gashasp was sent to bring/present someone before the world‑king.
segments.s672-g335: که introduces a relative clause (“who/that”). همشهری من is the noun phrase ‘my fellow‑citizen/townsman’ (possession shown by من). ببند is a classical verbal form of بستن here meaning ‘has (been) shut up / has been bound’; in context it reports that he has been closed/kept inside. اندرست (‘is within’) states the result/location: “is inside.” The clause thus defines the ‘someone’ of the previous sentence.
segments.s672-g336: به زندان is the locative phrase ‘in/into prison’ (به + زندان). ببیم is an archaic exclamatory/1st‑person reporting form equivalent to ‘behold/I see’ (here giving the speaker’s observation). و is the coordinating conjunction ‘and’. گزند اندرست is a predicate: گزند ‘harm’ + اندرست ‘is within/there is’, meaning ‘harm is present’ or ‘he is in danger/being harmed’. Together this reports the speaker’s observation about the captive’s condition.
segments.s672-g513: شما را is the object (شما = 'you', را marks it as definite/object). بویم is an archaic/poetic first‑person‑plural verb form placed after the object; here it functions as the main action 'we will (make/place)' but its exact lexical cognate is irregular in the glosses. اندرین پیشرو is an adverbial phrase 'in/among this vanguard' (اندرین = 'in this', پیشرو = 'vanguard/forefront') and links to بویم as its locative complement.
segments.s672-g514: نشانیم is a first‑person‑plural verb 'we will seat/place' taking the previously marked object (شما را). برگاه is the place 'the royal court' that functions as the verb's complement. اوشاه is a reference to 'that king' (not a vocative here) and نو is the adjective 'new' modifying اوشاه.
segments.s672-g515: وگر is the conditional conjunction 'if' (or 'but if'). هیچ پستی کنید is the protasis 'you do any meanness/disgrace' — هیچ here negates/quantifies the following noun in the sense 'any/at all' (sense lists do not provide an exact 'any', so treated cautiously). کنید is the present/subjunctive/pluperfect form 'you do' (polite/plural). اندرین locates the action 'in this/among these'.
segments.s672-g516: شما را again marks the direct object 'you'. سپاریم is first‑person‑plural 'we will entrust/commit' (سپاردن). ایران‌زمین is the object of سپاریم, 'the land/realm of Iran' — the phrase means 'we will entrust Iran to you.'
segments.s672-g517: یکی گوشه‌ای is an indefinite locative phrase 'to some/a certain corner' (یکی = 'a certain', گوشه‌ای = 'a corner/secluded spot'). بس کنیم is a compound meaning 'let us be content/let us settle' or 'let us stop/withdraw' (بس = 'enough', کنیم = 'let us do'), and ازجهان specifies withdrawal 'from the world' (ازجهان = 'from the world').
segments.s672-g518: بیک سو is a phrase meaning 'toward one side/one direction' (سو = 'toward/side'); the exact lexical gloss for بیک in the provided senses does not match the idiomatic use here. خرامیم is first‑person‑plural 'we stride/pace proudly' and باهمرهان means 'with our companions/together' — together they describe the manner and company of the withdrawing action.
segments.s672-g519: بگفتار is a prepositional/relational phrase 'by the word/command of' or 'according to (the saying of)'. گستهم here appears to be a proper name (the addressee/actor whose word it is) rather than the verbal sense given in the glosses, so it is best read as a name in context. یکسر سپاه is an adverbial + noun 'the entire army' or 'the army all at once' (یکسر = 'entirely/at once', سپاه = 'army').
segments.s672-g520: گرفتند is the past tense third‑person plural 'they took/they seized' used here with an abstract object. نفرین is the object 'curse'. برام and شاه form the target of the curse; the supplied senses for برام do not align cleanly with the phrase as written, so its precise gloss in the list is ambiguous, while شاه is 'the king'. The segment thus reads 'they took a curse upon the king.'
segments.s672-g185: چنین گفت زان پس که هرگز بخواب = a prefatory clause: چنین گفت 'he said thus', زان پس 'after that/thereupon' (literal 'from that then'), and که introduces the reported clause. هرگز 'never' combines with بخواب (classical contraction of به خواب, 'in sleep/in a dream') to mean 'never, even in a dream'.
segments.s672-g186: نبینم رخ شاه با جاه و آب = main reported clause. نبینم is first-person negative (subjunctive/optative sense here: 'may I not see' / 'I shall not see'), رخ شاه is the direct object 'the king's face', and با جاه و آب is an instrumental/qualifying phrase 'with rank and with āb' — here آب is figurative 'splendor/glory'.
segments.s672-g187: هرآنگه که خسرو نشیند بتخت = temporal subordinate clause. هرآنگه که functions as 'whenever/when', خسرو نشیند is third-person present/habitual 'Khosrow sits', and بتخت is a fused/poetic form for بر تخت 'on the throne' (prepositional complement placed after the verb noun).
segments.s672-g188: پسرت آن گرانمایه نیکبخت = nominal phrase referring to 'your son' with two qualifying adjectives. پسرت is 'your son' (possessive suffix), آن is a demonstrative/emphatic 'that' (here marking the epithet), and گرانمایه and نیکبخت are adjectives 'precious/esteemed' and 'fortunate/blessed'. The clause is elliptical (verb is implied by the context).
segments.s672-g189: بفرمان او کوه هامون کنم = agentive prepositional phrase + result clause. بفرمان او 'by his command' sets authority, followed by object phrase (کوه) and a result or complement (هامون) with the verb کنم 'I will make/do' at the end — typical Persian verb-final order: 'I will make the mountain (into) Hamun.'
segments.s672-g190: بیابان زدشمن چو جیحون کنم = 'بیابان' (the desert) is the theme; زدشمن here reads as a fused expression meaning 'against/striking the enemy' (verbal idea bound to دشمن), چو 'like/as', and جیحون the river-name used as a simile; کنم again is the first-person verb 'I will make' (i.e. 'I will make the desert like the Jayhūn when it smites the foe'). Word order is subject/theme + modifying phrase + simile + verb-final.
segments.s672-g191: همی‌خواست تا بردرشهریار = 'he intended/was about to' (همی‌خواست), with تا introducing the purpose/infinitival clause 'to ...'. بردرشهریار is a fused locative 'بر در شهریار' meaning 'to/at the king's door' or 'before the king'.
segments.s672-g192: سرآرد مگر بی‌گنه روزگار = سرآرد ('to bring/produce/appear' here: 'to present/bring oneself') completes the purpose clause; مگر introduces an exception 'unless' and بی‌گنه روزگار is a personified phrase 'fate/time, innocent (or free of guilt)' — i.e. 'but for (innocent) fate' meaning that fate (chance) prevented the intended action.
segments.s672-g353: بدو گفت is a two-word sequence meaning 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him/unto it' precedes the verb گفت). هر کس که اخترشناس is a noun phrase with a relative clause: هر کس 'whoever/anyone', که introduces the relative clause, and اخترشناس is the compound noun 'star-knower' meaning 'astrologer'.
segments.s672-g354: بنزد تو آید is a locative + pronoun + verb sequence meaning 'comes to you' (بنزد = 'to the presence of', تو = 'you', آید = 'comes'). پذیرد سپاس is the reported directive: پذیرد (here third-person present/subjunctive used as an instruction/report) = 'accept/welcome', سپاس = 'thanks' (direct object).
segments.s672-g355: یکی پیرزن مایه‌دار is an indefinite noun phrase: یکی = 'a certain', پیرزن = 'old woman', مایه‌دار = 'wealthy'. ایدرست is a brief poetic/intensifying particle meaning 'indeed' / 'truly' attached after the noun phrase; the copula 'was' is omitted as typical in Persian.
segments.s672-g356: که introduces a relative clause ('who/for whom'); گویی here functions impersonally as 'it seems' / 'as if' (classical usage). مگر is an emphatic/hesitative particle ('perhaps' / 'as if'), دیده = 'eye', and اخترست is adjectival/predicative 'star-like' (literally 'is of a star' = 'starry' or 'like a star').
segments.s672-g357: سخن 'speech/words' followed by هرچ (هر چه) = 'whatever' and گوید = 'she says' (here reported as 'she spoke'); نیاید = 'does not come to pass / does not happen' and جز آن = 'except that' — the clause says nothing of what she utters comes true except the following phrase.
segments.s672-g358: بگوید here reports the content of her saying ('she says' / 'she would say'); بتموز = 'in Tammuz' (the month), رنگ = 'color/hue', and خزان = 'autumn' — the reported utterance is that in Tammuz there will be the color/appearance of autumn.
segments.s672-g359: چو + بشنید (written here as چوبشنید) is a temporal clause meaning 'when he heard' or 'when he came to hear'. گفتارش = 'his speech/utterance' (object of the hearing). آیین گشسب = 'the custom/manner of Gushasp' or 'Gushasp's practice' — a noun phrase placed after the temporal clause that ties into what follows.
segments.s672-g360: هم اندر زمان is a temporal/frame adverbial ('also/then, at that time'); کس = 'someone' (an agent or messenger), فرستاد = 'he sent' (past tense, verb at the end as is common), و = 'and', اسب = 'a horse/steed'. The clause means that at that time he sent someone and a horse.
segments.s672-g529: نهادند is a 3rd-person plural past tense verb (they placed/put). پس is a temporal adverb 'then' linking this action to the preceding events. داغ is a noun here meaning a physical mark/brand; بر is the preposition 'on'; چشم شاه is a genitive construction 'the king's eye' (چشم + noun modifier شاه). Word order is typical classical Persian: verb first (نهادند) then the temporal adverb and the object phrase.
segments.s672-g530: شد is the simple past/ perfective 'became' (3rd-person singular). آنگاه is a temporal adverb 'then/thereupon'. آن شمع رخشان is a noun phrase 'that radiant candle' (آن determiner + شمع noun + رخشان adjective), and سیاه follows as a predicate adjective describing the state the candle has become (i.e. 'turned black'). This is a compact poetic clause: verb (شد) + time (آنگاه) + noun phrase + result adjective.
segments.s672-g531: ورا is the object pronoun 'to him/him' placed before the verb (archaic/poetic placement). همچنان is an adverb 'still' or 'as before'. زنده is an adjective 'alive' describing ورا, and بگذاشتند is the past plural verb 'they left' (they allowed/left him in that state). The sequence shows object + adverb/adjective + verb (common in older Persian).
segments.s672-g532: زگنج is a fused prepositional phrase (ز = از) meaning 'from the treasury'. آنچ is an archaic relative pronoun 'that which/whatever' and is the direct object of برداشتند. بد پاک are adverbial modifiers placed before the verb to emphasize manner/intensity (پاک here functions as 'entirely/wholly', and بد is an older adverbial/poetic modifier). برداشتند is the past plural verb 'they took/removed'. Overall: 'from the treasury they took whatever, wholly/entirely.'
segments.s672-g533: چنینست is a fixed copular phrase 'it is thus' / 'such is'. کردار is the noun 'deed' or 'action', followed by چرخ بلند, where چرخ (the cosmic 'wheel' = heaven/fate) is modified by بلند 'high/lofty'. The phrase reads 'Such is the deed/action of the lofty Wheel'—a compact, proverbial statement about fate.
segments.s672-g534: دل is the direct object 'heart'. اندر is the locative preposition 'in/within'. سرای here is the noun 'house/dwelling' (classical usage; note modern lemma lists may show other senses). سپنجی is an adjective 'spongy' (or 'like a sponge'). مبند is the negative imperative 'do not bind/close' (m- prefix + بند, classical negative command). The clause literally: 'Do not bind the heart within a spongy house.'
segments.s672-g535: گهی is an adverb 'sometimes'. گنج is the noun 'treasure'. بینیم is the 1st-person plural present/formal-subjunctive poetic form 'we see/behold' (poetic present used for general statements). ازوگاه is a temporal phrase 'from that time/then' and رنج is the noun 'suffering/sorrow' that is linked to what follows. The clause sets up alternating fortunes: sometimes treasure is seen, …
segments.s672-g536: براید is an older third-person singular present/impersonal form meaning 'it arises/comes' or 'comes upon' (here intransitive 'sorrow comes'). بما is a dative 'upon us/to us'. بر is the preposition 'upon/on'. سرای سپنج repeats 'the spongy house/dwelling'; together the phrase means 'sorrow comes upon us into the sponge-like dwelling'—a poetic way to say hardship follows good fortune and settles in one's home.
segments.s672-g297: The verb شد (past ‘became’) precedes the phrase in inverted/poetic word order: 'آیین گشسب' (the custom/way of Gushasp) is the subject, 'اندران' ('among them') is a locative adverbial, and 'چاره جوی' is a compound participial adjective meaning 'seeking a remedy'. Together this reads: 'Gushasp's practice became (i.e. he began) a seeking-of-a-remedy among them.'
segments.s672-g298: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that/so that'; 'آن کار را' is the definite object ('that affair/the matter' + object marker را). 'چون' here asks 'how', and دهد is the present/past-form verb 'gives' (here 'might give' in sense). 'رنگ وبوی' is a noun phrase 'color and scent/appearance'. The clause means 'how he might give that affair color and scent.'
segments.s672-g299: بدو ('to him') + گفت (here past 'said') forms the verbal clause 'he said to him'. The following vocative 'کای شاه گردن‌فراز' addresses the addressee: کای = 'O' (vocative), شاه = 'king', گردن‌فراز = an epithet 'proud/lofty‑necked'. The whole phrase is a direct address embedded after the reporting verb.
segments.s672-g300: This clause describes the timing: 'سخنهای بهرام' = 'Bahram's words/speeches' and 'چون شد دراز' = 'when/how they became long' (چون = 'how/when', شد = 'became', دراز = 'long'). In context it marks that the speaker spoke as Bahram's speech lengthened.
segments.s672-g301: The phrase uses subject-first poetic order: 'همه خون من' ('all my blood') is the subject and 'جوید' (present/poetic verb 'seeks') governs it; 'اندر نهان' is an adverbial 'within/in secret'. The clause reads 'all my blood seeks secretly' (object understood from context).
segments.s672-g302: نخستین زَ من گشت خسته روان is compact classical phrasing: 'نخستین' ('at first') + the poetic preposition ز (from) + من ('me') yields 'at first from me'; گشت (became/turned) + خسته روان ('weary‑soul', i.e. 'my spirit became weary'). The line means: 'At first my spirit became weary because of it.'
segments.s672-g303: مرا ('me', object) + نزد او ('near him/in his presence') gives the location; 'پای کرده' is a participial construction 'having made (the) foot' or 'having fixed (my) foot' — here meaning 'set/secure (my) feet' — followed by the imperative ببند ('bind' / 'fasten'). Overall it is an instruction: 'fasten/bind me and set me near him.'
segments.s672-g304: فرستی is an optative/subjunctive or hortative form 'you may send' (here 'send me'); مگر functions as a conditional/qualifier ('if perhaps' / 'unless' in the available options); باشدت is an archaic subjunctive 'may it be to you' and سودمند is the predicate adjective 'useful/profitable'. Together: 'Send me, if it may be useful to you.'
segments.s672-g57: همی is the habitual/continuous marker (old/poetic) marking an ongoing or repeated action. بر here works as a verbal prefix/element attached to جهاند (poetic verb) to form the sense 'display/make known'; the compound effectively means 'display to the world' or 'make famous'. یلان سینه اسب is a compressed noun phrase (poetic omission of the ezāfe) meaning 'heroes with chests like horses' (i.e. 'horse‑chested heroes'). Word order is typical of Ferdowsi's poetry: modifiers follow the head without the full modern connective.
segments.s672-g58: که here introduces the reason/explanation ('for/because'). تا functions in a purposive/continuative sense in poetry ('so that' / 'as long as'); من is the 1st‑person pronoun 'I'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of', followed by the name phrase بهرام پور گشسب ('Gushasp, son/descendant of Bahram') indicating lineage. The whole clause gives the speaker's reason or claim of descent.
segments.s672-g59: بنو is the tribal/clan element 'Banu' (the sons/house of ...), here the group that the speaker promises to raise. در جهان is a prepositional phrase 'in the world'. شهریاری کنم is a verb phrase with کنم (present/subjunctive 1st person) used optatively/futurally: 'I will make/establish (a) kingship/sovereignty'. The verb is transitive: 'I will make (the Banu) possess rulership in the world.'
segments.s672-g60: تن خویش is 'his/one's own body' with خوی + possessive ش (here written separately) functioning as 'own' (poetic/older orthography); را is the definite object marker, and یادگاری کنم repeats the same verb کنم to mean 'I will make (it) a memorial/memento' — i.e. 'I will leave my body as a memorial.' The structure is Subject (understood I) + object + result noun + کنم (optative/future).
segments.s672-g61: خردمند is an adjective 'wise'; شاهی (literally 'kingship' or 'regal power') here names the ruling authority ('a wise rulership' or 'wise ruler' in poetic usage). چو = 'like/as'. نوشین روان is an epithet: نوشین 'pleasant/delightful' modifying روان 'soul/spirit' — 'sweet‑souled' or 'pleasant in spirit.' The phrase poetically characterizes the ruler/royalty.
segments.s672-g62: بهرمز is a proper name (the person affected). بدی روز is a noun phrase 'a bad/evil day' (بدی = harm/evil, روز = day) used idiomatically for a day of misfortune. پیری جوان is an elliptic, contrastive phrase 'old age [for] youth' — i.e. 'turning youth into old age.' The clause as a whole says that Bahramz was given a disastrous day that deprives youth of its vigour (poetic compression of cause and effect).
segments.s672-g63: بزرگان کشور is a noun phrase 'the great ones of the country' (ezāfe omitted in poetic style). ورا = 'to him' / 'for him' marks the beneficiary or target. یاورند is 3rd person plural present 'they are supporters / they help.' The clause means 'the realm's grandees are his supporters.'
segments.s672-g64: اگر is the conditional 'if'. یاورانند‌گر is a fused/poetic conditional phrase meaning roughly 'if they act as helpers' (modal/conditional). کهترند is a present predicate 'they are fewer / they are inferior' — the line concedes that even if they are supporters, they may be few in number or lesser in strength. The sequence is conditional + concessive comment.
segments.s672-g265: همه (everyone) is the general subject; پیش تو is an adverbial phrase ‘before you / in your presence’. تن (body) is the direct object placed before the verbal complex بکشتن دهیم. بکشتن is the infinitive ‘to kill’ and دهیم is first‑person plural present/subjunctive ‘we give / let / will cause’; together this periphrasis means ‘we will allow/offer our bodies to be killed’ (literally ‘give the killing of [our] bodies’).
segments.s672-g266: سپاسی (praise/thanks) is the noun object; بران کشتگان is a prepositional phrase ‘upon/for those slain’ modifying سپاسی. برنهیم is an archaic 1st‑person plural form (graphically one unit here) that functions as a verb meaning ‘we shall place/bestow’ (likely from بر + نهیم). The line therefore says they will bestow praise on the slain; note the poetic/archaic verb formation.
segments.s672-g267: بدیشان (to them) is a prepositional phrase marking the indirect object; چنین گفت خسرو shows the common epic inversion where the verb phrase (‘thus said’) precedes the speaker (خسرو). که is the subordinating particle introducing the reported content clause which begins with من.
segments.s672-g268: پرازبیمم is an archaic fused expression meaning ‘I am full of fear / I greatly fear’ (literally ‘full of fear am I’); از marks the source/cause (‘of/from the shah’). و آن انجمن links ‘and that assembly’ as the second object of fear. The clause is a simple present/emotive statement in reported speech.
segments.s672-g269: اگرپیش combines the conditional اگر (‘if’) with پیش (‘in the presence of’) giving a conditional temporal/location setting. آذر گشسب is a proper name (Azar‑Gushasp) and این سران (‘these chiefs/heads’) is the subject that will carry out the action in the apodosis. Word order places the conditional phrase first, as typical for protasis clauses.
segments.s672-g270: بیایند is a 3rd‑person plural present/subjunctive/potential ‘they may come / they come’ used here in the protasis. The conjunction و links the next nominal phrase سوگندهای گران (‘heavy/solemn oaths’), where گران intensifies the seriousness of the oaths; these oaths are what the chiefs will perform.
segments.s672-g271: خورند here is the verb that goes with سوگندهای گران (they ‘take’ or ‘swear’ the oaths); the form is 3rd‑person plural present/subjunctive. و connects that action to the next result clause. مرا (m + را split in the manuscript) is the object ‘me’; یکسر is an adverb ‘entirely/altogether’; ایمن کنند is a 3rd‑person plural causative ‘they make [me] safe/secure’. Together: ‘they swear and make me completely secure.’
segments.s672-g272: که introduces a subordinate/result clause ‘so that / with the result that’. پیمان من is the subject/possessed noun ‘my covenant/pledge’. زان سپس is a temporal/relative phrase ‘from that time on / thereafter’. نشکنند is 3rd‑person plural negative present/subjunctive ‘they do not break’ (ن‑ prefix + شکند), giving the purpose/result: they will not break my covenant after that.
segments.s672-g193: همی‌یادکرد is a past narrative verb built from یاد کردن with the prefixed particle همی- (a poetic continuous/emphatic marker); here it functions as 'he mentioned' or 'he had (it) written'. این is the proximal demonstrative 'this' (the thing just referred to). به نامه درون is a prepositional phrase: به marks the relationship (here roughly 'into/with/regarding'), نامه = 'letter', and درون specifies the letter as internal/private ('within'). Together the phrase means 'he mentioned/put this into a private letter.'
segments.s672-g194: فرستاده is a noun 'a messenger'. آمد is the simple past 'came/went' (in narrative it reports the action). سوی طیسفون is a destination phrase: سوی = 'toward' and طیسفون = 'Ctesiphon'. The clause reads literally 'a messenger came toward Ctesiphon' (in context implying the letter was sent by messenger).
segments.s672-g195: ببازارگان combines the preposition بـ (to/for) with بازارگان ('merchants'), so 'to the merchants' or 'before the merchants'. گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (here reported speech). مهر درم is a compact noun phrase: مهر 'favour/affection' and درم 'a dirham (coin)'; idiomatically it suggests a favour bought for a dirham (i.e. favour for money).
segments.s672-g196: چو introduces a temporal/conditional clause 'when/if'. هرمزد is the deity Ahura Mazda. بیند is a present-form of دیدن used in narrative sense 'sees' (or 'should see' in an optative/remote use). بپیچد is a present-subjunctive/optative form of پیچیدن with the prefix بـ (here 'turns away' or 'will turn away'); the verb follows the condition set by چو. زغم is a prepositional-source/cause phrase 'from/because of sorrow' and gives the reason for turning.
segments.s672-g197: چو is the conditional/temporal 'when/if'. خسرو is the proper name 'Khosrow' used here as 'the king/sovereign'. نباشد is the negative present/subjunctive form of بودن 'to be' (literary usage meaning 'is not / does not remain'); ورا (to + را fused) functions as 'for him'/'to him' marking the beneficiary/affected person; یاروپشت is a compound noun 'friend and backer' (ally, support). The clause as a whole says 'if/when Khosrow is not there for him as friend and support.'
segments.s672-g198: ببیند is a present/future-oriented form of دیدن ('will meet/experience' in this context). ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (source or cause). من is the first-person pronoun 'me' (object here, 'from me'). روزگار functions as 'fate' or 'hard times' rather than simple 'time', and درشت is an adjective 'harsh/rough' modifying روزگار. Grammatically the verb precedes the prepositional phrase: 'he will experience harsh fate from me.'
segments.s672-g199: چو again introduces a temporal/conditional clause 'when/if'. آزرمها is a plural noun (here contextually 'the oppressions/harms' or 'the arrogant ones') serving as the object of the action. بر زمین is the locative phrase 'upon the ground/earth' (بر = on/upon, زمین = earth). برزنم is a first-person verb form (ending -م) expressing the speaker's action 'I will cast/lay low' (poetic/archaic verbal formation); together the clause says 'when I cast the harms/oppressors to the ground.'
segments.s672-g200: همی is the emphatic/continuous particle (here giving a resolute tone: 'indeed/still'). بیخ means 'root' or 'base'. ساسان refers to the Sasanians (either the name Sasan or the Sasanian house) and combines with زبن (an archaic form of 'زبان', 'tongue' or 'speech') to mean 'the Sasanian tongue/line/speech' (a metonym for their lineage or language). برکنم is the first-person verb 'I will uproot/tear out' (برکنیدن), placed at the end as the main verb: 'I will uproot the Sasanian tongue from its root.'
segments.s672-g369: This chunk sets up a noun + relative clause before the verb appears in the next segment. میـان اندران (literally “among them”) is an adverbial phrase marking location; مرد is the head noun “man”; کو is an archaic relative particle introducing the clause “who/whom”; را is the object marker attached to that relative; زشاه is a prepositional phrase ‘by/from the king’ that names the agent of the action which will be expressed by the verb in the following segment.
segments.s672-g370: رهانید is the past verb completing the relative clause established previously (“(the king) freed/released (him)”). The conjunction و links this clause to the next independent clause. با او بیامد is a normal past construction meaning “he came with him” (با = with; او = him; بیامد = came). به راه is a locative phrase “on the road / on the way.”
segments.s672-g371: به پیش = “before / in front of / in the presence of”; زن فالگو is a noun phrase ‘the fortune-telling woman’ (زن = woman, فالگو = fortune-teller); برگذشت is a past verb meaning “passed by / passed.” The whole segment is a simple past clause describing motion relative to that woman.
segments.s672-g372: This short clause contains نگه کرد, literally “made/looked (a look)” — نگه here is a noun ‘gaze/looking’ used with کرد to form the verb phrase ‘he looked / cast a glance.’ واندر is the conjunction + preposition ‘and into / and among,’ and گذشت is again the past verb ‘passed.’ The first lexical item بمهتر is irregular/archaic here and likely functions as the direction/target of the look (its exact lexical sense is uncertain in the supplied lexemes), so I mark it as uncertain; the grammar is: [adverb/phrase] + نگه کرد (he looked) + واندر گذشت (and he passed into/among them).
segments.s672-g373: بدو = “to him / unto him” marking the indirect object of گفت. پیرزن is the subject ‘the old woman.’ گفت is the past verb ‘said.’ The remainder of the sentence (کین مرد کیست) is reported speech introduced immediately after; syntactically here these words form the content of what she asked.
segments.s672-g374: که introduces a subordinate clause explaining the reason or content of the question (“that / who”). از زخم او = “because of his wound” (از = from/because of; زخم = wound; او = his). برتو = a prepositional/locative phrase meaning “upon you / concerning you.” باید is the modal ‘must/ought,’ and گریست here functions as a subordinate verbal form meaning “to weep / one should weep” — together باید گریست = “one must weep.” The clause reads: “that, because of his wound, you ought to weep.”
segments.s672-g375: پسندیده is an adjective ‘chosen / approved / prized’ modifying the noun that follows. هوش تو = “your intellect / wit” (possessive follows the noun, as is common in classical Persian). بردست اوست is a prepositional statement: بردست = ‘in the hand/power of’ and اوست = ‘it is his / it is in his hand.’ The structure states that the addressee’s intellect is at the other person’s disposal/power.
segments.s672-g376: که introduces an explanatory clause (“for / that”). The following sequence is condensed and uses archaic/poetic forms: مه is here an archaic possessive meaning “my” (not present among the supplied sense choices), مغز = “brain,” بادش = “his/its wind” or “fortune/mood” (i.e., influence), بتن = ‘on (the) body’ and در مه پوست = ‘in my skin’ (در = in; پوست = skin). Grammatically this clause gives a reason or a descriptive image: the speaker is saying that, by some influence or fate, the brain/mind and body/skin are implicated — the exact lexical items are poetically compact, so several words are archaic possession or idiom rather than literal vocabulary.
segments.s672-g257: نگر is an imperative 'look/see' commanding attention. تا introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that' / 'in order that'); نداری here functions as a nominal notion of 'not having' or 'lack' (an older/poetic way to express 'you do not have'), with هراس as the thing lacked; از marks the source/agent of that fear and گزند is the noun 'harm'. Word order places the thing lacked (هراس) before its source phrase (از گزند).
segments.s672-g258: بزی is a simile/noun 'as a goat' or 'be a goat' used exhortatively (be like a goat). شاد و آرام are two postposed adjectives linked by و describing that goat-like state. دل ارجمند is a nominal phrase 'heart (دل) + esteemed/noble (ارجمند)' meaning 'a noble heart' — the adjective follows the noun in the usual Persian order.
segments.s672-g259: زمانی is a temporal adverb 'at times/for a while' setting the frame. بنخچیر ('to/for the spoil/booty') functions adverbially/goal-like. تازیم is a 1st‑person‑plural present/poetic form 'we rush/gallop/charge' (the -یم ending marks 'we'); اسب follows as the object or instrument 'horse/steed' (word order in epic verse often puts the verb before or around its object).
segments.s672-g260: Again زمانی is temporal. نوان ('the young/young men') is the subject/noun phrase. پیش + آذر is a prepositional phrase 'before/in the presence of Azar' (آذر read here as a proper name). کشسب is a problematic/manuscript form; in context it behaves like a verb 'gain/obtain' (probably from کسب), but the form is irregular/uncertain — the line thus says the youths before Azar win or take gains.
segments.s672-g261: برسم here functions as a prepositional/nominal phrase 'according to the custom/usage' (in this context 'برِ رسمِ نیاکان' = 'by the custom of the ancestors'); the printed senses do not list this nominal reading, so its precise sense is irregular in the provided list. نیاکان is the genitive 'ancestors'. نیایش کنیم is a compound verbal idea 'let us perform/worship/pray' — کنيم is 1st‑person plural hortative/subjunctive 'let us do / we shall do' used in poetry.
segments.s672-g262: روان را places the definite object 'the soul' before the verb (روان = 'soul/spirit', را = definite object marker). به یزدان is the indirect object 'to/for God' (یزدان = God). نمایش کنیم is 'let us show/present' — نمایش (a noun 'display') + کنيم makes the verbal idea 'to display/present', with کنيم again marking hortative/first‑person plural.
segments.s672-g263: گراز شهر ایران is a genitive phrase 'the boar (of the) city of Iran' (گراز = wild boar/brute). چو introduces a simile 'like/as' and pairs with the numeral 'سیصد هزار' ('three hundred thousand') to compare or enumerate — poetic inversion places the numeral after چو.
segments.s672-g264: گزند is the noun 'harm/damage' and here is the semantic subject of نشیند; تو را ('you') is marked with the object/topic marker را and precedes the verb, a normal Persian order in poetry. بر is the preposition 'upon/on', نشیند is 3rd‑person present/subjunctive 'may ... sit/alight' (optative use) and سوار is a vocative 'O rider' or an appositive 'rider' addressing the addressee. The whole clause reads as an optative wish about harm alighting upon the rider; the line is somewhat elliptical and epic poetry often omits explicit negatives or auxiliaries, so nuance can vary.
segments.s672-g385: This is a compact past‑tense clause: 'یکی' is an indefinite pronoun 'someone/a man' placed before the object 'نامه' (letter), and 'بنوشت' is an archaic/poetic past form of نوشتن 'wrote' (the b‑ prefix appears in older epic usage). 'نزدیک شاه' is a locative phrase 'near the king' following the verb.
segments.s672-g386: The conjunction 'که' introduces reported speech ('saying that...'). 'این را' is the demonstrative object 'this' plus the definite/object marker 'را'. 'کجا' is the interrogative 'where' placed before the verb, and 'خواستستم' is first‑person past 'I asked/desired' (classical past form). 'به راه' is a prepositional phrase 'on the road' (به used locatively/purposefully).
segments.s672-g387: 'نبایست' is an archaic modal/impersonal negative meaning 'it ought not / it was not fitting'; it is followed by the verbal noun/infinitive 'کردن' ('to do') forming the impersonal construction. 'ز زندان رها' is a short prepositional phrase 'released/from prison' where 'رها' functions as 'free/let loose', so the whole chunk means 'to set free from prison'.
segments.s672-g388: 'که' here gives a causal/explanatory sense 'for/because'. The predication 'این بتر از تخمه اژدها' uses the comparative 'بتر از' = 'worse than'; 'این' is the demonstrative subject 'this/he', 'تخمه' here means 'offspring/spawn' (not literal seed), and 'اژدها' is 'dragon', so the line reads 'for he was worse than the dragon's brood.'
segments.s672-g389: 'همی‌گفت' is the iterative/imperfect past 'kept saying' or 'would say' used in epic narration; the verb comes first here, followed by the subject 'شاه'. 'این سخن' = 'these words/this speech' is the object, and 'با رهی' is a poetic use of با with a person meaning 'to/with the wayfarer' (i.e., 'he said these words to the traveller').
segments.s672-g390: Here 'رهی' (the wayfarer) is marked by 'را' as topic/emphasis, and 'نبد' is an archaic negative past copula meaning 'was not / did not have'. 'فر' is the noun 'royal glory/radiance' and 'شاهنشهی' (شاهنشاهی) qualifies it as the glory belonging to imperial kingship. Together: 'the wayfarer lacked the royal glory of empire.'
segments.s672-g391: 'چوآید' is a temporal conjunction 'when he came' (classical narrative use of چو + verb). 'بفرمای' is an authoritative verb form (from فرمودن/فرمان دادن) here meaning 'he ordered/commanded'. 'تا' introduces the subordinate clause indicating the timing/purpose 'so that / when', and 'درزمان' is a temporal locative 'in the time / at the appointed time'.
segments.s672-g392: The verb 'ببرد' (subjunctive/optative of بردن with the b‑ prefix) means 'they should take/carry away'. 'بخنجر' is an instrumental phrase 'with a dagger'. 'سرش' is 'his head' (سر + the clitic ش = his), and 'بدگمان' follows as an adjective 'the suspected / suspicious one' modifying the object; poetically the instrument precedes the object here.
segments.s672-g249: ز کرمان = 'from Kerman' (ز as the poetic preposition). چو + بیورد is a temporal clause: چو here means 'when' and بیورد is an archaic/past verb ('came/brought'); گرد و سوار is a poetic coordination meaning 'a host (gathering) and horsemen' — the verb چو بیورد introduces that contingent. Note the compressed, somewhat inverted epic word order where the verb phrase (چو بیورد) serves to introduce the arriving groups.
segments.s672-g250: ز شیران = 'from (among) the شیران' (here metaphorical 'the brave/lion-like men'). چون = 'like/as' introducing the comparison, followed by the proper names سام اسفندیار ('Sam Esfandiyar') as an example of such a hero.
segments.s672-g251: یکایک = 'one by one' (adverbial). بخسرو = the directional phrase 'to the king' (ب- prefix + خسرو). نهادند is the past/plural of نهادن 'to place, set' used here idiomatically with روی to mean 'set/turn (their) face(s)'; روی = 'face' (direction) — literally 'one by one to-the-king set they their faces.'
segments.s672-g252: سپاه و سپهبد = 'the army and the spahbed (general)'. همه = 'all' / 'altogether' qualifying both items. شاهجوی is a deverbal/adjectival compound 'seeking the king' (i.e. intent on the sovereign); here it is a predicate attributing the attitude to the army and spahbed.
segments.s672-g253: همی‌گفت = an imperfective/past-continuous poetic form 'kept saying' or 'would say'. هرکس here functions as 'each/every one' (everyone present kept repeating the same). که‌ای is a compressed vocative/exclamatory combination (که + ای) addressing the prince: 'O (you who are) ...', followed by پور شاه 'son of the king' (vocative).
segments.s672-g254: تو را = 'you' with the object marker را (stresses the object). زیبد is an archaic/present-stem verb meaning 'befit' / 'suit' (here 3rd-person singular: 'it suits you'). این تاج و تخت وکلاه = 'this crown and throne and cap' (a tricolon listing regalia) — the whole clause says that these honors befit the addressee.
segments.s672-g255: از ایران و از دشت = 'from Iran and from the plain' (repeated از for rhythm/emphasis). نیزه وران is a compound: نیزه-وران = 'spearmen' (نیزه 'spear' + a suffix forming agent nouns and plural), so the phrase lists spearmen coming from those regions. The words are split for metrical reasons but function as a single noun phrase.
segments.s672-g256: ز خنجر گزاران = 'from the dagger-(wielding) ones' (خنجر 'dagger' + گزاران forming an agent plural: 'those who use daggers'); this is another grouped noun. و جنگی سران = 'and the war-leaders' where جنگی is an adjective/agentive 'warlike / fighter' modifying سران 'leaders'. Together the segment continues the catalogue of warrior-types.
segments.s672-g321: This is a noun phrase built up in classical Persian order: یکی ('a certain') introduces an indefinite; مرد ('man') is the head noun; بد ('bad') is a post-nominal adjective (classical Persian frequently places adjectives after the noun); بسته is a past participle meaning 'bound/taken' and attaches to the noun as a secondary descriptor; از شهر اوی is a prepositional phrase 'from his town' with اوی an archaic 3rd-person pronoun ('him/his') referring back to a possessor. The whole phrase describes 'a certain man, bad and bound, from his town.'
segments.s672-g322: به + place phrase marks motion/direction: به زندان اندرون means 'into/ toward the prison's inner part'; شاه modifies زندان as a genitive/attributive ('the king's prison' or 'the inner prison of the king'). چاره جوی is a present-participial phrase 'seeking a remedy' (چاره = remedy; جوی = seeker/search), placed after the place phrase to indicate purpose or the man's activity. There is no separate finite verb here; the participle carries the purposive sense.
segments.s672-g323: چو (here fused as چوبشنید) is the archaic conjunction 'when'; بشنید (here combined as چوبشنید) is the past tense 'he heard'. کیین is an archaic temporal/intonational particle ('then/when' or an intensifier) linking the clause to what follows. گشسب is the proper name (Gushasp) and سوار ('horseman' or 'rider') is an appositive title following the name. The whole segment sets up the subject: 'When he heard (this), Gushasp, the rider...'.
segments.s672-g324: همی‌رفت is an imperfective/past habitual form meaning 'was going' or 'set off' (the prefix همی marks continuous or habitual aspect in classical Persian). خواهد here functions as a modal/future auxiliary giving intention or immediacy ('would/will'), so the combination conveys that he immediately set off or intended to go. سوی کارزار is a directional prepositional phrase 'toward the battlefield' completing the motion verb.
segments.s672-g325: کسی را is the direct object ('someone') marked by the definite/object marker را (here used after an indefinite to mark the person being sent). ززندان is an ablative phrase 'from the prison' (ز- = از); به نزدیک اوی is a prepositional phrase 'to near him / to his vicinity' where اوی is the archaic 3rd-person pronoun 'him' or 'his' used as the goal. Note the object phrase and its adjuncts precede the verb فرستاد, consistent with Persian's verb-final tendency.
segments.s672-g326: فرستاد is the past tense verb 'sent.' The following cluster کای مهتر نامجوی names the person involved: کای here is a title (Kay) rather than a vocative, مهتر is an honorific 'chief/elder', and نامجوی functions as a proper name or epithet. The sequence identifies the addressee or named figure connected with the sending; in classical Persian names and titles often follow the verb for emphasis or apposition.
segments.s672-g327: زشهرت is a prepositional/ablative phrase ('from/on account of his fame') formed with ز (classical variant of از) + شهرت. یکی مرد زندانیم is an indefinite noun phrase 'a certain man of the prison' (here 'زندانیم' functions as 'prisoner' or 'one attached to the prison'). The whole segment gives the topical reason or circumstance ('because of his fame, a certain prisoner...').
segments.s672-g328: نگویم is the negative present/subjunctive or jussive form often used by the poet to mean 'I will not say' or 'let me not tell' (here the provided sense list did not include 'say/speak', so a direct-match sense is not available). همانا is an emphatic particle 'verily/truly'. که introduces a subordinate clause 'that' and خود ('self') is an emphatic pronoun 'we ourselves/himself' modifying دانیم. دانیم is a present-tense first-person-plural form from 'دانستن' meaning 'we know.' Together: 'I will not say — truly, that we ourselves know.'
segments.s672-g121: تو گویی که گفتارش از دفترست is an embedded clause introduced by تو گویی (literally “you might say” or “one would say”), where تو is the 2nd-person pronoun and گویی is an optative/present-form of گفتن used here to report a hypothetical or commonly held remark. که introduces the content clause; گفتارش is گفتار (speech) with the 3rd-person enclitic -ش referring back to the woman just spoken of; از + دفترست (دفتر + ست) expresses origin/“from the book/record” — دفترست is a poetic/compounded form meaning something like ‘from a register/book’. Word order is typical Persian (topic/particle + verb + que-clause + noun phrase).
segments.s672-g122: بدانش is an imperative/hortative construction: بدان (’know this’) plus the enclitic -ش pointing back to the woman or the preceding statement; it functions as a discourse marker meaning “know (this).” ز here is the poetic/genitive-from used in comparisons (‘than’/‘from’); جاماسب is the proper name Jamasp; نامی is an adjective ‘renowned/famous’; ترست is the old-style comparative/copular form (تر + ست = ‘is more’), so the phrase states a comparison: ‘she is more famous than Jamasp.’
segments.s672-g123: چو here is the temporal subordinating particle ‘when’. بهرام را uses the Persian object/affected-marker را after the name to mark Bahram as the person concerned (‘to Bahram’ / ‘for Bahram’). آن is the demonstrative ‘that’ referring back to what displeased him. نیامد is the compound negative/past literal ‘did not come’ but used idiomatically with پسند (پسند as noun) to mean ‘it did not please’ or ‘was not to his liking.’ The whole clause thus reads ‘when that did not please Bahram…’.
segments.s672-g124: همی‌بود is the habitual/continuous past (would be / used to be) marker; ز introduces the cause or source (‘because of’ / ‘from’); آواز خواهر is a genitive noun phrase ‘the sister’s voice’ (آواز = voice/song, خواهر = sister); نژند is an adjective meaning ‘base/lowly/worthless’ describing the voice or his attitude toward it. Together: ‘he would be contemptuous because of his sister’s voice,’ i.e. he would scorn her singing.
segments.s672-g125: This is a string of epithets: دل تیره = ‘a dark/bleak heart’ (دل = heart/mind; تیره = dark/ominous) and اندیشه دیریاب = ‘a hard-to-find / long-sought thought’ or ‘rare thought’ (اندیشه = thought; دیریاب = rare / long-lost). The two noun phrases characterize his inner state before the action of the next clause.
segments.s672-g126: همی is an imperfective/habitual marker here (‘was/again and again’ or simply continuous past). تخت شاهی is a compound noun ‘the throne of kings’ (تخت = throne; شاهی as adjectival/nominal ‘kingly/kingship’). نمودش is from نمود (to appear/show) with enclitic -ش meaning ‘to him’ or ‘it’ depending on context — here: ‘showed him’ or ‘was shown to him’. بخواب = به + خواب ‘in sleep’ i.e. ‘in a dream.’ The clause therefore says that, in sleep, the throne of kings was shown to him.
segments.s672-g127: چنین گفت is the formulaic ‘thus he said’; چنین = ‘thus/so’, گفت = past of گفتن ‘he said’. پس is an adverb here meaning ‘then/thereupon’. کین سرای is a genitive phrase ‘the house/place of vengeance’ (کین = vengeance/enmity; سرای = house/place); سپنج is a poetic noun appended to characterize that house (literally ‘sponge/absorbent’ in the provided senses) — in context it functions as a descriptive or metaphorical epithet attached to the place of vengeance.
segments.s672-g128: نیابند is the present/subjunctive/negative form meaning ‘they do not/find not’ or ‘will not find’ (third person plural); جویندگان is the plural noun ‘seekers/searchers’; جز = ‘except’ or ‘only’; به رنج = ‘by/with hardship’ (به here marks means/agency and رنج = hardship/toil). The clause states that seekers will not find it except with toil.
segments.s672-g9: یکی is an indefinite subject placed before the verb meaning 'someone/a certain one.' بانگ برزد is a poetic past-tense verbal phrase (برزد = an archaic/poetic verb meaning ‘utter/resound’) meaning 'uttered a cry'; بر آن‌کس is a prepositional phrase 'to/at that person' (بر = toward); که گفت is a relative clause modifying آن‌کس (که = 'who/that'), with گفت intended as past 'said' (the content of that saying follows).
segments.s672-g10: که introduces the reported clause ('that' / the words he spoke). با دخمه تنگ is a prepositional phrase ('in/with the narrow tomb') where تنگ qualifies دخمه. باشید is the present-subjunctive / polite imperative 2nd- or 3rd-person plural meaning 'be' or 'remain' used as a command here; جفت is the predicative complement 'paired/together' (literally 'a pair'), placed after the verb.
segments.s672-g11: که here begins a subordinate/relative clause linked to the surrounding speech; با شاه is 'with the king.' باشد is a present-subjunctive/optative form of 'to be' (expressing where someone ought/can be), and کجا is the interrogative adverb 'where' placed before the noun for emphasis; پهلوان is the noun 'champion/hero' (the expected subject of the clause). The word order shows inversion (کجا پیش‎میشود + noun) typical of poetic/older Persian.
segments.s672-g12: نشستند is the simple past, 3rd-person-plural 'they sat.' بآیین ('بـ' + آیین) is an adverbial phrase 'in accordance with custom / ceremonially' and روشن‌روان is a compound adjective 'bright‑souled / with cheerful faces' describing their manner; together they form an adverbial phrase modifying نشستند. Note the prefixed preposition/particle بـ attaches directly to آیین.
segments.s672-g13: مرا here functions as the dative/possessive 'for me' (older Persian word order places the possessor before the predicate). تخت زر is the noun phrase 'golden throne' (تخت + زر as modifier), and باید is an impersonal/modal 'it is necessary / I must have.' و links the next clause; بسته شاه is an adjectival phrase 'the king (being) bound/fastened' where بسته is an adjective/participial form modifying شاه; the verb of the second clause is elliptical and completed by the following مباد in the next segment.
segments.s672-g14: مباد is a negative jussive particle meaning 'may it not be' (optative). این گمان 'this suspicion' is the first object of that wish; the second مباد … این کلاه repeats the negative jussive for parallelism. کلاه here is used figuratively (hence 'trick/insult/embarrassment' rather than a literal hat). The verb ('be' / 'exist') is understood/omitted after مباد, a common elliptical construction.
segments.s672-g15: گزین کرد is a two-word verbal sequence: گزین (choose/select) + کرد (do/make) forming the past tense 'he selected.' ز ایران is the source phrase 'from Iran' (ز = poetic 'from'), and ده و دو هزار is the numeral 'ten and two thousand' = twelve thousand, placed after the source phrase as the object amount.
segments.s672-g16: This segment lists names/titles followed by سوار ('rider / horseman'). جهانگیر is a proper name/epithet and برگستوانور appears as an obscure compound/name; the final سوار functions as a head noun ('horsemen') applying to the preceding names — a common appositional/listing structure in epic diction.
segments.s672-g1: چو at the start is the temporal/linking particle 'when'. The phrase lists two people: گودرز (a proper name) then و 'and' plus چون introducing the second name (here functioning like 'and/also') followed by رستم پهلوان 'Rostam the champion' (پهلوان is an epithet). Word order is poetic but simply names who perform the following action.
segments.s672-g2: بکردند is the past transitive verb 'they did/caused'. رنجه here is a noun 'suffering/trouble' and is the thing they caused. برین is the preposition بر + the demonstrative این ('on/ upon this'), and روان is the target 'soul/spirit'. Poetic word order places the verb up front; meaning: 'they caused suffering upon this soul.'
segments.s672-g3: ازان پس = 'after that / thereupon'. کجا شد is an idiomatic past question 'what became (of).../where did ... go?' with شد as 'became'. بهاماوران is a plural noun 'the rumor‑mongers / sowers of discord'.
segments.s672-g4: ببستند is past 'they bound/tied'. پایش = پای + ـش 'his foot' (object). ببند here functions in the line as a nominal/metrical form meaning 'band/fetter' (used with گران 'heavy') — together 'ببند گران' = 'heavy fetters' (poetic compression). So the clause means 'they bound his feet with heavy fetters.'
segments.s672-g5: کس here means 'no one' (an indefinite negation). آهنگ in this construction means 'aim/attempt' (آهنگ کردن = to aim for). این تخت شاهی = 'this throne of kingship'. نکرد is the past negative 'did not do', so the whole clause: 'no one attempted this royal throne.'
segments.s672-g6: جز از introduces an exception/qualification 'without exception/from ...'. گرم و تیمار ایشان = 'their fervor and care/vigilance' (گرم = zealous/fervent, تیمار = care/solicitude). نخورد here uses the sense 'befall/come upon' (i.e., 'did not fail to suffer/meet with'); the clause means roughly 'who did not fail to meet with their zeal and vigilance' — i.e., anyone who tried met their zealous watchfulness.
segments.s672-g7: چو = 'when'. گفتند is the past verb 'they said' introducing reported speech ('they said to Rostam'). با + name = 'to/with Rostam' (here 'to Rostam'). ایرانیان = 'the Iranians / countrymen' is the speaker group. (Note: the provided sense list for گفتند did not include the verb sense 'they said', so its sense index is marked -1.)
segments.s672-g8: که introduces the embedded question 'who/that'. هستی here is the 2nd‑person present of 'to be' ('are you' = thou art) used in direct address; the supplied senses treat هستی as a noun ('being'), so its verb sense is not in the list (marked -1). تو = 'you'. زیبای = vocative/adjectival 'beautiful/fair one'. تخت کیان = 'the Kayanian throne' (تخت = throne, کیان = the Kayan dynasty). The clause is the Iranians' question addressed to Rostam.
segments.s672-g137: بخوردند is a past tense, 3rd‑person plural verb (خوردن) here meaning ‘they drank/ate’; with می (wine) it means ‘they drank’. بر یاد او is a prepositional phrase ‘in/for his memory’ (بر = on/in, یاد = memory, او = him) placed before the object for poetic word order. چند می = ‘some/ a few wines’ (چند modifies می, approximate quantity).
segments.s672-g138: که introduces the following wish/clause; آباد is the adjective ‘prosperous/inhabited’ and بادا is an optative particle ‘may it be’ (poetic wish). بوم ری = ‘the bōm (homeland) of Rey’ (بوم = homeland, ری = Rey). برو is a small poetic/deictic element here attached to بوم (poetic emphasis: ‘that bōm of Rey’); its exact function is archaic/poetic.
segments.s672-g139: کزان = ‘from which/from that’ (ک+زان) marks origin. بوم is ‘land/homeland’. خیزد is a 3rd‑person singular present/habitual or future‑oriented verb from خاست/خیزیدن ‘to rise/appear’ (‘will rise/arise’ here). سپهبد is the noun ‘spahbed’ (military title) and چوتو = چو + تو ‘as/like you’ (a comparative following the noun). Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect: origin phrase + verb + title + simile.
segments.s672-g140: فزون آفریناد ایزد is an optative/jussive formula: فزون ‘more/abundant’ modifies the verb آفریناد ‘(may) create’, with ایزد ‘God’ as the subject (poetic word order). چو تو = ‘like you’. Together: ‘May God create many more like you.’ آفریناد here is a wish (optative) rather than simple narration.
segments.s672-g141: پراگنده گشتند is a past, 3rd‑person plural construction: پراگنده ‘scattered/widely dispersed’ + گشتند ‘they became/turned’, meaning ‘they scattered/ were dispersed’. چون تیره شد is a temporal clause ‘when/since it became dark’ (چون here functioning as a temporal/conjunctive word; تیره شد = ‘became dark’). Word order places the result before the temporal clause for rhythm.
segments.s672-g142: سرمیگساران is a compound noun ‘the chief/reigning revelers’ (leaders among the drinkers). ز is the poetic preposition ‘from/by’. می‌خیره شد is a resultative/passive‑like phrase: می‌خیره (خیره شدن) ‘to be transfixed/stare in astonishment’ with شد making it past/resultant – ‘were transfixed/stunned by the wine’. The clause means the principal revelers were stupefied by the wine.
segments.s672-g143: چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when’. برزد is an archaic past verb here meaning ‘struck/was struck’ or ‘fell upon’. سنان آفتاب بلند is a descriptive noun phrase ‘the spear (سنان) of the high/tall sun’ (آفتاب بلند qualifies سنان) — in poetry possessor/epithet sequences like this are common. The clause reads as ‘When the spear of the high sun struck…’.
segments.s672-g144: شب تیره گشت = ‘the night became dark’ (شب = night, تیره = dark, گشت = became/turned). از درفشش نژند: از marks source/cause (‘from/of’), درفشش = ‘his banner/standard’ (درفش with pronominal suffix implied in the poem), and نژند is used predicatively ‘base/worthless/sullied’ — literally ‘from his banner it became base’, i.e. the banner’s radiance/renown was blemished. The clause attributes a darkening or sullied glory to the banner.
segments.s672-g41: Word order is Subject (بزرمهر) + verb with attached indirect object (داد + ش = 'gave him'). یکی پرهنر is a post-verbal noun phrase meaning 'a certain/skilled one' (one = یکی, modified by adjective پرهنر). دادش is the fused form of داد + ـش (gave to him).
segments.s672-g42: که introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/possibility ('so that' / 'that'); کین (vengeance) is the object, پدر is its possessor, and بازخواهد is a future/predictive verb ('will return/exact'), here idiomatically 'will avenge'. مگر at the end expresses contingency/possibility (classical use meaning roughly 'perhaps' or 'unless').
segments.s672-g43: نگه کرد is a compound-like expression where نگه ('gaze') plus کرد ('did') yields 'looked/observed'. زرمهر is the subject; کس را ندید is a negated past clause with را marking the (definite) object: 'he saw no one.'
segments.s672-g44: که introduces a relative clause modifying 'کس' ('who/that'). The prepositional phrase با تاج بر تخت (with crown on the throne) precedes the verbal predicate سزید ('deserved'); سزید is past-tense classical verb 'to deserve' (third-person). شاهی is a noun 'kingship' functioning as the complement of سزید.
segments.s672-g45: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'; the phrase places زرمهر as subject, افگند is a past verb 'cast/threw', and مهر (here 'affection' or 'favour') is the object. Word order is slightly inverted but common in classical Persian: 'When Zarmehr cast affection upon the king...'.
segments.s672-g46: گردان سپهر ('the revolving/circling heaven') is the subject; خواند is the past verb 'uttered/sang/said'. آفرین is the acclamation 'bravo' (the thing uttered). برو here is a classical particle placed with the acclamation (an intensifying/vocative flavor in context); overall: 'the circling heavens cried
segments.s672-g47: ازو بند برداشت is literal word order 'from-him fetter removed': ازو (a prepositional/clitic pronoun 'from him') precedes the noun بند ('fetter, bond'), and برداشت is a past tense verb 'removed, lifted'. تا introduces a purpose clause ('so that/in order that'). کار خویش combines کار ('affair, business') with خوی + ش, where خوی is the base and ش is the enclitic possessive 'his/her/its' — together meaning 'his own affairs'.
segments.s672-g48: بجوید کند shows a classical/periphrastic ordering: بْجوید is the 3rd-person present/subjunctive form of جستن 'to seek', and کند (the verb 'to do/make' in present) follows as an auxiliary-like element; together they convey 'he set about seeking' or 'he sought' (a common poetic construction where the two verbs combine). تیز is an adverbial/qualifying adjective 'swift/quick' modifying the action, and بازار خویش is 'his market/business' (بازار 'market' + خوی + ش = 'his own').
